docs.title: Agentic AI Ecosystem
docs.text:
A hand-curated taxonomy of the agentic AI tooling landscape — 11 domains, 280 categories, 284 projects. Categories are functional slots; projects are evidence that a slot exists.

Browse via the sidebar on the left, use the search bar, or click a domain below.

docs.title: Domains
docs.text:
• Agents

    Agent runtimes, frameworks, harnesses, and surfaces that define, host, or coordinate individual and multi-agent systems.

    26 subcategories

  • Builders & Platforms

    Platforms and products for building, deploying, and operating agents — the IDE and PaaS layer of the ecosystem.

    8 subcategories

  • Context, Memory & Data

    Everything that feeds agents with context, persistent memory, and structured data.

    3 subcategories

  • Dev Tools & Skills

    Tooling, reusable skills, and prompt assets that developers use to build and operate agents.

    3 subcategories

  • Ecosystem

    Meta-layer covering non-tool entities in the agentic AI landscape: organisations, destinations, and uncategorised items.

    3 subcategories

  • Frameworks & Runtimes

    Top-level domain for the software substrate that executes agents: frameworks, runtimes, harnesses, loops, and orchestration.

    8 subcategories

  • Interfaces

    User-facing surfaces through which humans interact with agents — frontends, workspaces, UX patterns, and annotation tools.

    4 subcategories

  • Models & Inference

    The model layer: base models themselves and the infrastructure that serves them to agents.

    2 subcategories

  • Observability & Evals

    Telemetry, evaluation, and operational monitoring for LLM and agent systems across their lifecycle.

    4 subcategories

  • Safety & Governance

    Controls, policies, and oversight mechanisms that keep agents safe, compliant, and accountable in production.

    6 subcategories

  • Tools & Integrations

    Tools, actions, and integration layers that connect agents to the outside world — MCP, standards, and action surfaces.

    3 subcategories

Alternate views: Hub-Spoke · Graph · JSON · GitHub

docs.title: Agents
docs.text:
Agents

Agent runtimes, frameworks, harnesses, and surfaces that define, host, or coordinate individual and multi-agent systems.

Everything whose primary concern is the agent itself — how it is built, executed, composed with others, and exposed to users. Subcategories split by architectural role (framework vs harness vs runtime), composition style (single, multi-agent, networks), and surface (CLI, desktop GUI, frontend). Excludes underlying models, tools, and infrastructure.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Agent Clusters (1 proj) — Deployments that run many related agents as a managed cluster sharing state, scheduling, or lifecycle.
  • Agent Collaboration (1 proj) — Environments where multiple agents cooperate on shared tasks via roles, dialogue, or negotiation.
  • Agent to Agent (1 sub, 3 proj) — Protocols, channels, and libraries that let independent agents address and message one another directly.
  • Autonomous (1 sub) — Long-running agents that pursue goals with minimal human supervision once initialised.
  • Autonomous Agent Creation (1 proj) — Tools where an agent generates, configures, or spawns new agents autonomously.
  • Backend Platforms (1 proj) — Hosted or self-hostable backends that provide APIs and infrastructure for running user-defined agents.
  • CLIs/Toolkits (1 sub) — Command-line tools and SDKs for defining, running, and debugging agents from a terminal.
  • Computer Use (1 sub, 1 proj) — Agents that drive a full computer via screen, keyboard, and mouse to accomplish user tasks.
  • Desktop GUIs (1 proj) — Native desktop applications that package agents as installable GUI software.
  • Development (1 proj) — Agents whose purpose is to write, modify, and ship software end-to-end.
  • Domain Specific (1 sub) — Agents tailored to the workflows and data of a particular industry or knowledge domain.
  • Frameworks (1 sub) — Libraries for defining agents, tools, memory, and control flow in application code.
  • Frontends (1 proj) — User-facing chat and control UIs for interacting with agents built elsewhere.
  • Harnesses (1 sub) — Outer control loops that drive a model through tool use, planning, and iteration on a task.
  • Memory (1 sub) — Memory subsystems that give agents persistent, recallable state across turns and sessions.
  • Multiagent (2 sub) — Systems whose defining abstraction is many agents reasoning or acting in parallel.
  • Networks (1 proj) — Open networks where agents from different authors or vendors can be discovered and invoked.
  • Personification (1 proj) — Agents given a persistent persona, voice, or identity as their primary design feature.
  • Purpose Specific (1 sub, 1 proj) — Agents built around a single well-defined purpose or task type.
  • Simulations & Worlds (4 sub) — Agent-populated simulations of worlds, societies, or characters used for research, gameplay, or scenario exploration.
  • Synthesised Perspectives (2 proj) — Orchestrating multiple LLM personas to surface a synthesised range of viewpoints on a question.
  • Task Specific (1 sub) — Agents built to execute a single, well-defined task rather than serving as general-purpose assistants.
  • Team Formation (1 proj) — Tools that assemble agent teams by selecting roles, skills, and configurations for a given goal.
  • Teams (1 proj) — Pre-configured agent teams targeting a specific workflow such as data science or research.
  • Tools (1 sub) — Tooling and integrations that agents call as capabilities, grouped by function.
  • Vision (1 proj) — Agents whose primary input is live visual streams — camera, video, or screen — for perception and action.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Xata Agent

docs.title: Agent Clusters
docs.text:
Agents / Agent Clusters

Deployments that run many related agents as a managed cluster sharing state, scheduling, or lifecycle.

Clustered agent runtimes where agents are provisioned and operated as a group rather than invoked ad-hoc. Typically a platform or service that handles spawning, identity, and persistence for a fleet of agents under one roof. Distinct from Multiagent (which focuses on reasoning patterns) and Networks (cross-org discovery).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • IntentKit

docs.title: Agent Collaboration
docs.text:
Agents / Agent Collaboration

Environments where multiple agents cooperate on shared tasks via roles, dialogue, or negotiation.

Runtimes and frameworks whose core abstraction is collaborative problem-solving between agents — role assignment, turn-taking, debate, joint planning. Typically a library or sandbox rather than a transport protocol. Differs from Agent-to-Agent (which is about the communication channel) and Teams (preassembled crews).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AgentVerse

docs.title: Agent to Agent
docs.text:
Agents / Agent to Agent

Protocols, channels, and libraries that let independent agents address and message one another directly.

The communication substrate for inter-agent messaging — discovery, addressing, message format, and session semantics between otherwise separate agents. Usually a protocol or SDK rather than a full orchestration framework. Collaboration patterns built on top belong under Agent Collaboration.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Experiments (1 sub) — Experimental setups exploring direct agent-to-agent communication and emergent behaviours.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentChattr
  • ConnectOnion
  • OpenAgents

docs.title: Experiments
docs.text:
Agents / Agent to Agent / Experiments

Experimental setups exploring direct agent-to-agent communication and emergent behaviours.

Research-style experiments in which two or more agents interact directly, often probing protocols, negotiation, or voice-to-voice exchange. In-scope: demos and prototypes. Out-of-scope: production multi-agent frameworks.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Voices (1 proj) — Experimental voice-based protocols for agent-to-agent communication.

docs.title: Voices
docs.text:
Agents / Agent to Agent / Experiments / Voices

Experimental voice-based protocols for agent-to-agent communication.

Research projects where agents negotiate a more efficient voice channel when they detect they're both machines — e.g. switching to non-human audio encodings. Distinct from standard voice interfaces because the counterparty is another agent, not a human.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Gibberlink

docs.title: Autonomous
docs.text:
Agents / Autonomous

Long-running agents that pursue goals with minimal human supervision once initialised.

Agents designed to operate open-endedly toward a goal, re-planning and acting without step-by-step human prompting. Subcategories specialise by vertical (e.g. Trading). Distinct from task-specific or harness-driven agents that expect tight human-in-the-loop interaction.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Trading (1 proj) — Autonomous agents that execute financial trading strategies end-to-end.

docs.title: Trading
docs.text:
Agents / Autonomous / Trading

Autonomous agents that execute financial trading strategies end-to-end.

LLM-driven agents that analyse markets and place or simulate trades with minimal human intervention. Typically standalone apps combining data ingestion, strategy reasoning, and broker/exchange APIs. Sits under Autonomous because the agent closes the loop without a human in the loop.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AI-Trader

docs.title: Autonomous Agent Creation
docs.text:
Agents / Autonomous Agent Creation

Tools where an agent generates, configures, or spawns new agents autonomously.

Meta-level systems in which an agent's output is another agent — skill generation, self-extension, or dynamic agent bootstrapping. The interface is typically a runtime or SDK that exposes agent-creation as a first-class action. Excludes human-driven low-code agent builders.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • BankrBot Skills

docs.title: Backend Platforms
docs.text:
Agents / Backend Platforms

Hosted or self-hostable backends that provide APIs and infrastructure for running user-defined agents.

Server-side platforms (often with a studio UI) that handle agent definition, execution, state, and API exposure as a managed service. Typically LLMOps-adjacent products like Dify. Distinct from Frameworks (libraries) and Frontends (chat UIs).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Dify

docs.title: CLIs/Toolkits
docs.text:
Agents / CLIs/Toolkits

Command-line tools and SDKs for defining, running, and debugging agents from a terminal.

CLI-first developer toolkits for agent construction and execution — scaffolding, local runs, tool wiring. Vendor-branded CLIs live under the Vendor child. Excludes coding-assistant CLIs (which belong under Development) and full GUI harnesses.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Vendor (1 proj) — First-party agent CLIs and toolkits shipped by model vendors.

docs.title: Vendor
docs.text:
Agents / CLIs/Toolkits / Vendor

First-party agent CLIs and toolkits shipped by model vendors.

CLI-based agent harnesses released directly by model providers (e.g. Qwen, Anthropic, OpenAI) as the canonical way to drive their models agentically. Distinct from third-party CLIs that wrap multiple vendors' models.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Qwen Code

docs.title: Computer Use
docs.text:
Agents / Computer Use

Agents that drive a full computer via screen, keyboard, and mouse to accomplish user tasks.

Agents whose action space is general desktop/OS control — screenshots, clicks, keystrokes — rather than narrow APIs or browsers alone. Includes local harnesses that wrap vision-language models to operate a machine. Browser-only automation and narrow RPA belong elsewhere.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Local Computer-Use Harnesses (3 proj) — Harnesses that run computer-use agents locally on the user's own machine.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • open-computer-use

docs.title: Local Computer-Use Harnesses
docs.text:
Agents / Computer Use / Local Computer-Use Harnesses

Harnesses that run computer-use agents locally on the user's own machine.

Projects that let an LLM drive the local OS — keyboard, mouse, screen, shell — rather than a sandboxed remote VM. Typically Python apps wrapping a VLM and OS automation libraries. Distinct from cloud-hosted computer-use services.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Claude Computer Use Demo
  • Open Interpreter
  • Self-Operating Computer

docs.title: Desktop GUIs
docs.text:
Agents / Desktop GUIs

Native desktop applications that package agents as installable GUI software.

Installable Mac/Windows/Linux apps whose primary surface is a desktop GUI for interacting with one or more agents. Distinct from web Frontends and terminal CLIs. The focus is packaging and UX, not the underlying agent logic.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Hello Halo

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
Agents / Development

Agents whose purpose is to write, modify, and ship software end-to-end.

Coding agents that take engineering tasks — implement features, fix bugs, refactor, open PRs — usually via a harness over a code workspace. Examples like OpenHands sit here. Excludes IDE copilots focused on inline completion and generic frameworks.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OpenHands

docs.title: Domain Specific
docs.text:
Agents / Domain Specific

Agents tailored to the workflows and data of a particular industry or knowledge domain.

Vertical agents whose design is shaped by domain constraints (biotech, legal, finance, etc.) rather than generic task-solving. Subcategories split by domain. Differs from Task Specific, which groups by the kind of task performed rather than the field it operates in.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Biotech (1 proj) — Agents tailored to biotech and life-sciences research workflows.

docs.title: Biotech
docs.text:
Agents / Domain Specific / Biotech

Agents tailored to biotech and life-sciences research workflows.

Domain-specific agents for biology, genomics, and biomedical research — e.g. orchestrating bioinformatics tools and literature reasoning. Usually libraries or research platforms grounded in domain tool APIs. Sibling to other verticals under Domain Specific.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Biomni

docs.title: Frameworks
docs.text:
Agents / Frameworks

Libraries for defining agents, tools, memory, and control flow in application code.

Developer-facing frameworks used to build agents programmatically — agent loops, tool schemas, state, handoffs. Typically imported as a library rather than run as a service. Open-source exemplars live under the Open Source child; distinct from hosted Backend Platforms.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Open Source (1 proj) — Open-source agent frameworks usable as libraries for building custom agents.

docs.title: Open Source
docs.text:
Agents / Frameworks / Open Source

Open-source agent frameworks usable as libraries for building custom agents.

General-purpose frameworks for defining agents, tools, and loops, released under an open-source licence. Distinct from vendor-locked or proprietary frameworks. Typically a Python or TypeScript library.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Hermes Agent

docs.title: Frontends
docs.text:
Agents / Frontends

User-facing chat and control UIs for interacting with agents built elsewhere.

Web or app frontends that sit in front of an agent backend — chat panels, mission-control dashboards, session views. The agent logic and models are assumed to live behind the UI. Distinct from Desktop GUIs (native apps) and full Backend Platforms.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OpenClaw Mission Control

docs.title: Harnesses
docs.text:
Agents / Harnesses

Outer control loops that drive a model through tool use, planning, and iteration on a task.

Execution harnesses that wrap an LLM with a scaffolding loop — observe, plan, act, reflect — often with tool access and workspace mounts. Typically a runnable binary or CLI rather than a library. Frameworks provide building blocks; harnesses provide the whole loop.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • CLIs (2 sub) — Command-line harnesses that wrap LLMs into interactive coding or task agents.

docs.title: CLIs
docs.text:
Agents / Harnesses / CLIs

Command-line harnesses that wrap LLMs into interactive coding or task agents.

CLI-first agent harnesses (e.g. Claude Code, Aider-style tools) that run an agent loop in the terminal with shell and file access. Children split by vendor-first-party vs third-party. Distinct from GUI or IDE integrations.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Third Party (1 proj) — Third-party CLI agent harnesses not shipped by a model vendor.
  • Vendor (1 proj) — First-party CLI agent harnesses shipped by model vendors for their own models.

docs.title: Third Party
docs.text:
Agents / Harnesses / CLIs / Third Party

Third-party CLI agent harnesses not shipped by a model vendor.

Terminal-based coding/agent harnesses built by independent teams that target multiple models via provider APIs. Contrast with vendor CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini CLI) that are tied to a specific model provider.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Crush

docs.title: Vendor
docs.text:
Agents / Harnesses / CLIs / Vendor

First-party CLI agent harnesses shipped by model vendors for their own models.

Terminal coding agents published by the company behind the underlying model (e.g. Gemini CLI, Claude Code). Distinct from third-party CLIs which are model-agnostic and built by independent developers.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Gemini CLI

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Agents / Memory

Memory subsystems that give agents persistent, recallable state across turns and sessions.

Components dedicated to agent memory — episodic logs, semantic stores, knowledge graphs, summarisation layers — exposed to agents as a read/write interface. Graph Builders live as a child. Distinct from general-purpose vector databases, which sit under Context/Data.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Graph Builders (1 proj) — Libraries that construct and maintain knowledge graphs as agent long-term memory.

docs.title: Graph Builders
docs.text:
Agents / Memory / Graph Builders

Libraries that construct and maintain knowledge graphs as agent long-term memory.

Tools that extract entities and relations from agent interactions and persist them as a queryable graph for recall. Typically a Python library plus graph DB backend. Sibling to vector-store and episodic-memory approaches under Memory.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Graphiti

docs.title: Multiagent
docs.text:
Agents / Multiagent

Systems whose defining abstraction is many agents reasoning or acting in parallel.

Frameworks and experiments centred on multi-agent dynamics — large-scale simulations, swarm prediction, emergent collaboration — rather than the plumbing of pairwise messaging. Children cover large-scale experiments and swarm prediction. Agent-to-Agent protocols and cluster runtimes are separate slots.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Large Scale Experiments (1 proj) — Frameworks for running multi-agent experiments at population scale — thousands to millions of agents.
  • Swarm Prediction (1 proj) — Using collective agent swarms to generate forecasts or predictions.

docs.title: Large Scale Experiments
docs.text:
Agents / Multiagent / Large Scale Experiments

Frameworks for running multi-agent experiments at population scale — thousands to millions of agents.

Simulation platforms designed to scale agent populations far beyond typical crew sizes, for agent-based modelling and emergent-behaviour studies. Typically GPU-accelerated differentiable simulators. Distinct from small production crews and from Swarm Prediction (which is forecasting-focused).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AgentTorch

docs.title: Swarm Prediction
docs.text:
Agents / Multiagent / Swarm Prediction

Using collective agent swarms to generate forecasts or predictions.

Projects that aggregate the outputs of many agents into a prediction signal, drawing on wisdom-of-crowds dynamics. Distinct from generic large-scale experiments in that the goal is a forecast artefact, not behavioural study.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • MiroFish

docs.title: Networks
docs.text:
Agents / Networks

Open networks where agents from different authors or vendors can be discovered and invoked.

Directories, marketplaces, and protocol networks exposing many third-party agents behind a shared discovery and invocation layer. The unit is the network itself, not a single cluster. Distinct from Agent Clusters (single-operator fleets) and Agent-to-Agent (raw transport).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • agent.ai

docs.title: Personification
docs.text:
Agents / Personification

Agents given a persistent persona, voice, or identity as their primary design feature.

Projects where the agent is presented as a character — name, backstory, consistent voice — rather than a neutral tool. Typically companion-style or branded assistants. Distinct from role-based collaboration setups where personas are ephemeral scaffolding.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Bub

docs.title: Purpose Specific
docs.text:
Agents / Purpose Specific

Agents built around a single well-defined purpose or task type.

Narrow agents whose scope is one job (e.g. penetration testing) rather than a general assistant or domain generalist. Children enumerate the specific purposes. Differs from Domain Specific (defined by field of application) and Task Specific (defined by task shape).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Penetration Testing (1 proj) — Agents purpose-built to perform offensive security and penetration testing tasks.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • INKOS

docs.title: Penetration Testing
docs.text:
Agents / Purpose Specific / Penetration Testing

Agents purpose-built to perform offensive security and penetration testing tasks.

Autonomous or semi-autonomous agents that plan and execute pentest workflows — recon, exploitation, reporting — wrapping security tooling. Typically delivered as a standalone app or platform. Distinct from general-purpose coding or ops agents.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Pentagi

docs.title: Simulations & Worlds
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds

Agent-populated simulations of worlds, societies, or characters used for research, gameplay, or scenario exploration.

Covers frameworks and projects where LLM agents inhabit simulated environments — geopolitical wargames, societal models, generated worlds, and roleplay characters. In-scope: multi-agent simulation runtimes and world-generation tools. Out-of-scope: single-task autonomous agents or general multi-agent orchestration frameworks.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Character & Roleplay (3 sub) — Frameworks for character-driven roleplay agents, including persona definition, lore, and runtimes.
  • Geopolitical Simulation (2 proj) — Agent simulations of geopolitical actors, wargames, and international scenarios.
  • Societal Simulations (1 proj) — Multi-agent simulations modelling human societies, populations, and social dynamics at scale.
  • World Generation (1 sub, 1 proj) — Procedural or agent-driven generation of simulated 3D/2D worlds and environments.

docs.title: Character & Roleplay
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / Character & Roleplay

Frameworks for character-driven roleplay agents, including persona definition, lore, and runtimes.

Covers tools for defining roleplay characters, building lore/worldbuilding, and running persistent roleplay sessions. Distinct from societal or geopolitical simulations in that the focus is narrative persona fidelity rather than aggregate dynamics.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Character Definition — Schemas, formats, and tools for defining roleplay characters and personas.
  • Lore & Worldbuilding — Authoring tools for the lore, setting, and worldbuilding that surrounds roleplay characters.
  • Roleplay Runtimes — Runtime engines that execute character roleplay sessions with LLM-driven personas.

docs.title: Character Definition
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / Character & Roleplay / Character Definition

Schemas, formats, and tools for defining roleplay characters and personas.

Specifications and authoring tools that describe a character's traits, voice, backstory, and behaviour for use in roleplay runtimes. Sits upstream of the runtime that executes the character and distinct from world/lore authoring.

docs.title: Lore & Worldbuilding
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / Character & Roleplay / Lore & Worldbuilding

Authoring tools for the lore, setting, and worldbuilding that surrounds roleplay characters.

Frameworks for defining the shared universe — factions, history, locations, rules — that characters in a roleplay simulation inhabit. Distinct from per-character definition (which describes individual personas) and from runtimes (which execute the scene).

docs.title: Roleplay Runtimes
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / Character & Roleplay / Roleplay Runtimes

Runtime engines that execute character roleplay sessions with LLM-driven personas.

Servers or libraries that take character definitions plus lore and drive live multi-turn roleplay, managing memory, turn order, and scene state. Distinct from the static authoring layers (character definition, lore) which feed into them.

docs.title: Geopolitical Simulation
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / Geopolitical Simulation

Agent simulations of geopolitical actors, wargames, and international scenarios.

Platforms that model states, factions, and decision-makers as LLM agents for wargaming or policy exploration. Usually research-grade simulators with scenario scripting. Sibling to Societal Simulations (domestic/social) and World Generation (fictional worldbuilding).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Snowglobe
  • WargamesAI

docs.title: Societal Simulations
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / Societal Simulations

Multi-agent simulations modelling human societies, populations, and social dynamics at scale.

Environments where many LLM or rule-based agents interact as a synthetic society to study emergent social behaviour, opinion dynamics, or collective action. Typically research-oriented frameworks or sandboxes. Distinct from geopolitical simulation (state actors) and character roleplay (narrative personas).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OASIS

docs.title: World Generation
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / World Generation

Procedural or agent-driven generation of simulated 3D/2D worlds and environments.

Tools and engines that create the worlds agents inhabit — terrain, scenes, physics, and interactive assets — as opposed to populating them with societies or characters. Typically game engines, ML-powered generators, or MCP bridges into DCC tools.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • MCP (1 proj) — MCP servers exposing world-generation engines (e.g. Unreal) as tools for agents.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • ML-Agents

docs.title: MCP
docs.text:
Agents / Simulations & Worlds / World Generation / MCP

MCP servers exposing world-generation engines (e.g. Unreal) as tools for agents.

Model Context Protocol bridges that let agents drive 3D engines and DCC tools to generate or manipulate worlds programmatically. Specific to MCP as the integration surface, distinct from native SDK or plugin-based generators.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Unreal Engine MCP

docs.title: Synthesised Perspectives
docs.text:
Agents / Synthesised Perspectives

Orchestrating multiple LLM personas to surface a synthesised range of viewpoints on a question.

Tools that poll diverse agent personas (demographic, expert, or ideological) and aggregate their outputs into a composite perspective or deliberation. Typically a Python library or notebook pattern. Distinct from general multi-agent teams in that the point is viewpoint diversity, not task decomposition.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• LLM Council
  • Plurals

docs.title: Task Specific
docs.text:
Agents / Task Specific

Agents built to execute a single, well-defined task rather than serving as general-purpose assistants.

Parent slot for agents narrowly scoped to one workflow (e.g. product design, spec drafting). Contrasts with Domain Specific (broad vertical) and Purpose Specific (broad intent) by narrowing to a concrete task. Usually shipped as standalone apps or scripts.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Product Design (1 proj) — Agents specialised for product and UI/UX design workflows.

docs.title: Product Design
docs.text:
Agents / Task Specific / Product Design

Agents specialised for product and UI/UX design workflows.

Domain-specific agents that generate, critique, or iterate on product design artifacts — mockups, design systems, component layouts. Distinct from general coding agents by their focus on visual/design output and design-tool integration.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Superdesign

docs.title: Team Formation
docs.text:
Agents / Team Formation

Tools that assemble agent teams by selecting roles, skills, and configurations for a given goal.

Libraries and services that dynamically compose multi-agent teams — choosing which agents, what roles, and how they coordinate — rather than running pre-defined squads. Sits upstream of multi-agent runtimes; output is a team definition consumed by a framework like CrewAI or AutoGen.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Squad

docs.title: Teams
docs.text:
Agents / Teams

Pre-configured agent teams targeting a specific workflow such as data science or research.

Ready-made squads of specialised agents shipped as a unit for a defined workflow (e.g. data science pipelines). Distinct from Team Formation (which builds teams) and generic Multiagent frameworks (which provide primitives) — these are opinionated, domain-shaped teams.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AI Data Science Team

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
Agents / Tools

Tooling and integrations that agents call as capabilities, grouped by function.

Parent slot for discrete tools/capabilities exposed to agents — e.g. communications, search, actuation — rather than frameworks or runtimes. Children narrow by tool category. Out-of-scope: full agent frameworks or MCP servers (which live elsewhere).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Comms (1 proj) — Communication tools agents use to message, email, or otherwise talk to humans and other agents.

docs.title: Comms
docs.text:
Agents / Tools / Comms

Communication tools agents use to message, email, or otherwise talk to humans and other agents.

Tool-layer integrations that expose messaging channels (email, chat, MCP mail) as callable capabilities for agents. Usually MCP servers or SDK adapters. Sits under Agents → Tools alongside other tool categories an agent can invoke.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • MCP Agent Mail

docs.title: Vision
docs.text:
Agents / Vision

Agents whose primary input is live visual streams — camera, video, or screen — for perception and action.

Frameworks and runtimes for agents that reason over real-time vision (webcam, RTSP, screen capture). Typically built on VLMs plus streaming infrastructure. Distinct from Computer Use (which targets OS control) in that the emphasis is perception pipelines, not GUI actuation.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Vision Agents

docs.title: Builders & Platforms
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms

Platforms and products for building, deploying, and operating agents — the IDE and PaaS layer of the ecosystem.

Top-level segment covering the opinionated tooling stack sitting above raw frameworks: visual builders, hosted runtimes, deployment targets, and optimisation suites. Distinct from Frameworks (libraries) and Infrastructure (lower-level runtimes) by bundling UX and managed execution.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Automation (1 sub) — Automation platforms where agents orchestrate workflows across apps and services.
  • Deployment Platforms (1 sub) — Managed platforms for hosting and running agents in production.
  • Development Platforms (1 proj) — All-in-one environments for authoring, testing, and iterating on agents.
  • Engineering Platforms (1 proj) — Engineering-team-oriented platforms for building and operating agents as software systems.
  • On Device Agents (2 sub) — Platforms for running agents directly on end-user or edge devices rather than in the cloud.
  • Optimisation Platform (1 proj) — Platforms for evaluating, tuning, and optimising deployed agents and prompts.
  • Platforms (1 sub) — General-purpose agent platforms that don't fit a narrower builder/deployer/engineering slot.
  • SaaS Builders (3 sub) — Platforms where agents build or become end-user SaaS applications.

docs.title: Automation
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Automation

Automation platforms where agents orchestrate workflows across apps and services.

Workflow automation tools (Zapier-like, n8n-like) specifically oriented toward AI agent steps and LLM-driven branching. Distinct from pure deployment or development platforms by their event/trigger-driven, integration-heavy model.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Open Source (1 sub) — Open-source automation builders for wiring agents, APIs, and workflows without vendor lock-in.

docs.title: Open Source
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Automation / Open Source

Open-source automation builders for wiring agents, APIs, and workflows without vendor lock-in.

Self-hostable automation platforms where users assemble agent and integration workflows. Distinguished from proprietary SaaS automation by source availability and deployability on own infrastructure. Covers both headless engines and GUI-based flow builders.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • GUI Based (2 proj) — Open-source automation platforms offering a drag-and-drop graphical flow builder.

docs.title: GUI Based
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Automation / Open Source / GUI Based

Open-source automation platforms offering a drag-and-drop graphical flow builder.

Self-hostable automation tools with a visual canvas for building flows across apps, APIs, and LLMs. Distinguished from code-first open-source automation by the GUI authoring surface. Typically deployed via Docker with a browser-based editor.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Activepieces
  • Flowise

docs.title: Deployment Platforms
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Deployment Platforms

Managed platforms for hosting and running agents in production.

PaaS-style services that take a built agent and handle runtime concerns — scaling, scheduling, secrets, observability. Distinct from development platforms (where you author) and engineering platforms (which target engineering orgs).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Serverless (1 proj) — Serverless runtimes for hosting agents without managing long-lived infrastructure.

docs.title: Serverless
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Deployment Platforms / Serverless

Serverless runtimes for hosting agents without managing long-lived infrastructure.

Managed execution environments that run agent code on demand, billing per invocation or session. Typically offered as a service with SDKs or CLI deploy commands. Sits above raw cloud functions, adding agent-aware primitives like session state and long-running tasks.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Julep

docs.title: Development Platforms
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Development Platforms

All-in-one environments for authoring, testing, and iterating on agents.

Studios and IDE-like products where agents are designed, prompted, wired to tools, and tested — typically with visual canvases or integrated debugging. Distinct from deployment platforms (runtime hosting) and SaaS builders (end-user app output).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Coze Studio

docs.title: Engineering Platforms
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Engineering Platforms

Engineering-team-oriented platforms for building and operating agents as software systems.

Developer platforms that treat agents as code — version control, typed interfaces, CI, observability — aimed at software engineering orgs rather than citizen developers. Distinct from no-code SaaS builders and general development studios.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • VoltAgent

docs.title: On Device Agents
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / On Device Agents

Platforms for running agents directly on end-user or edge devices rather than in the cloud.

Runtimes and toolkits targeting SBCs, phones, and local hardware where the agent executes on-device. Distinct from cloud deployment platforms by their focus on local inference, constrained resources, and device-native integrations.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Android (1 proj) — Agent runtimes targeting Android handsets and tablets as the execution surface.
  • SBCs (1 proj) — Agent runtimes for single-board computers like Raspberry Pi and similar edge hardware.

docs.title: Android
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / On Device Agents / Android

Agent runtimes targeting Android handsets and tablets as the execution surface.

Frameworks and apps that let agents act on an Android device — reading screens, driving apps, and invoking system APIs. Distinguished from SBC targets by the mobile OS and app-permission model. Usually ship as APKs or on-device services.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent Device

docs.title: SBCs
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / On Device Agents / SBCs

Agent runtimes for single-board computers like Raspberry Pi and similar edge hardware.

Projects deploying agents to small Linux SBCs for edge, embedded, or hobbyist use. Distinguished from Android by running full Linux with GPIO/hardware access rather than a mobile app sandbox. Typically distributed as images or install scripts.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • be-more-agent

docs.title: Optimisation Platform
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Optimisation Platform

Platforms for evaluating, tuning, and optimising deployed agents and prompts.

Hosted suites that close the loop on agent performance — running evals, A/B tests, prompt/flow optimisation, and cost/quality tuning. Distinct from development platforms (authoring) and pure eval frameworks by their integrated, platform-level scope.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Coze Loop

docs.title: Platforms
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Platforms

General-purpose agent platforms that don't fit a narrower builder/deployer/engineering slot.

Catch-all for broad platforms spanning multiple stages of the agent lifecycle, including embodied-agent platforms. Use more specific sibling categories when applicable; this slot should stay small.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Embodied (1 proj) — Platforms for building agents embodied in physical or avatar form factors.

docs.title: Embodied
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / Platforms / Embodied

Platforms for building agents embodied in physical or avatar form factors.

Builder platforms targeting embodied agents — robots, avatars, or physical systems — rather than pure software workflows. Provides primitives for perception, actuation, or persona alongside LLM reasoning. Delivered as SDKs or managed services.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Fairo

docs.title: SaaS Builders
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / SaaS Builders

Platforms where agents build or become end-user SaaS applications.

Tools that generate full applications — backends, UIs, integrations — via agents, targeting no-code, visual-programming, or code-defined authoring styles. Distinct from development platforms that output agents themselves rather than agent-powered SaaS.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Code-Defined — SaaS agent builders where workflows are authored in code rather than visual canvases.
  • No Code — No-code SaaS builders that let non-developers assemble agents through forms and prompts.
  • Visual Programming (1 sub, 1 proj) — Visual node-based builders where agents and workflows are composed on a canvas.

docs.title: Code-Defined
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / SaaS Builders / Code-Defined

SaaS agent builders where workflows are authored in code rather than visual canvases.

Hosted platforms for building agents by writing code — typically Python or TypeScript — against a managed runtime. Sibling to no-code and visual-programming builders, trading accessibility for flexibility and versionability. Positioned between frameworks and fully managed no-code tools.

docs.title: No Code
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / SaaS Builders / No Code

No-code SaaS builders that let non-developers assemble agents through forms and prompts.

Hosted builders aimed at non-technical users, exposing agents via configuration UIs without requiring code or visual graph editing. Distinguished from visual programming by the absence of a node-and-edge canvas. Typically SaaS with template libraries and managed hosting.

docs.title: Visual Programming
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / SaaS Builders / Visual Programming

Visual node-based builders where agents and workflows are composed on a canvas.

SaaS and open platforms providing a graph editor of nodes and edges to wire LLM calls, tools, and control flow. Distinguished from pure no-code form builders by the explicit visual dataflow model. Often used for both automation and agent prototyping.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Workflow Focused (2 proj) — Visual SaaS builders optimised for multi-step workflows rather than conversational agents.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • n8n

docs.title: Workflow Focused
docs.text:
Builders & Platforms / SaaS Builders / Visual Programming / Workflow Focused

Visual SaaS builders optimised for multi-step workflows rather than conversational agents.

Visual-programming platforms whose primary unit is a linear or branching workflow chaining LLM and tool calls. Distinguished from conversational or agentic-loop builders by a workflow-centric execution model. Delivered as hosted SaaS with integration libraries.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Gumloop
  • MindStudio

docs.title: Context, Memory & Data
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data

Everything that feeds agents with context, persistent memory, and structured data.

Top-level domain covering how agents acquire, retain, and retrieve information: context assembly, memory stores, and data pipelines. Sits between raw storage/infra and the agent runtime. Children split the space into Context, Memory, and Data.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Context (6 sub, 2 proj) — Tooling for assembling, shaping, and optimising the context window sent to LLMs.
  • Data (6 sub) — Data layer feeding agents — embeddings, RAG, vector stores, search, and grounding.
  • Memory (7 sub, 4 proj) — Persistent memory systems that let agents remember across sessions, users, and time.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context

Tooling for assembling, shaping, and optimising the context window sent to LLMs.

Covers engines, optimisers, frontend capture tools, and visualisers that decide what goes into a prompt at inference time. Distinguished from Memory (persistent state) and Data (raw sources) by focus on the ephemeral context payload. Typical interface: libraries, IDE plugins, or middleware.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Context Optimisation (1 proj) — Tools that shrink, rank, or restructure context to fit window and cost budgets.
  • Engines (1 proj) — Context engines that orchestrate retrieval, assembly, and delivery of prompt context.
  • Frontend Tools (1 proj) — Frontend capture tools that grab UI, DOM, or screen state as context for agents.
  • Optimisation (2 sub) — Strategies for keeping context lean over the life of a conversation or agent loop.
  • Tools (1 sub) — General utilities for inspecting, editing, and managing context payloads.
  • Visualisation (1 proj) — Visualisers that render context windows, token budgets, and prompt structure.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• PromptX
  • WeKnora

docs.title: Context Optimisation
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Context Optimisation

Tools that shrink, rank, or restructure context to fit window and cost budgets.

Focused on pre-processing context before it hits the model — compression, pruning, reranking, deduplication. Distinguished from Engines (assembly runtime) and Optimisation (in-thread/cross-thread strategies) by targeting the payload itself. Delivered as libraries or middleware.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • context-mode

docs.title: Engines
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Engines

Context engines that orchestrate retrieval, assembly, and delivery of prompt context.

Runtime systems responsible for producing the full context payload at each LLM call, coordinating retrievers, rankers, and formatters. Distinguished from optimisation (a transform) and tools (user-facing utilities) by being the assembly runtime. Usually embedded as a library or service.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Contexto

docs.title: Frontend Tools
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Frontend Tools

Frontend capture tools that grab UI, DOM, or screen state as context for agents.

Browser or editor utilities letting users or agents pull live frontend state — DOM nodes, React trees, screenshots — into a prompt. Distinguished from backend context engines by operating at the UI layer. Typically shipped as browser extensions or client libraries.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • react-grab

docs.title: Optimisation
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Optimisation

Strategies for keeping context lean over the life of a conversation or agent loop.

Covers techniques like in-thread truncation and cross-thread compression that manage context across many turns. Distinguished from single-call Context Optimisation by focusing on conversation-scale dynamics. Children split by scope: within a thread vs across threads.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Cross-Thread Compression — Techniques that compress context across multiple conversation threads or sessions into compact summaries.
  • In-Thread Truncation — Within-conversation strategies that prune, summarise, or window context to fit model limits.

docs.title: Cross-Thread Compression
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Optimisation / Cross-Thread Compression

Techniques that compress context across multiple conversation threads or sessions into compact summaries.

Covers tools that summarise or distil history spanning many threads so agents carry forward global context efficiently. Distinct from In-Thread Truncation, which operates within a single conversation; this slot works across session boundaries.

docs.title: In-Thread Truncation
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Optimisation / In-Thread Truncation

Within-conversation strategies that prune, summarise, or window context to fit model limits.

Covers truncation, sliding windows, and mid-thread summarisation that keep a single conversation under token budgets. Distinct from Cross-Thread Compression by operating only on the live thread's messages.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Tools

General utilities for inspecting, editing, and managing context payloads.

Catch-all for user-facing context helpers — context managers, editors, inspectors — that don't fit engines or optimisers. Distinguished from engines (runtime) by being interactive tools. Typically CLIs, desktop apps, or IDE integrations.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Context Managers (1 proj) — Utilities for assembling, versioning, and injecting prompt context bundles into agents.

docs.title: Context Managers
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Tools / Context Managers

Utilities for assembling, versioning, and injecting prompt context bundles into agents.

Covers tools (e.g., dotai) that let developers curate reusable context packs — files, rules, snippets — and load them into agent sessions. Sits above raw prompt templating by managing context as structured, persistent artefacts.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • dotai

docs.title: Visualisation
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Context / Visualisation

Visualisers that render context windows, token budgets, and prompt structure.

Tools that let developers see what's inside a context window — tokens, segments, sources, costs. Distinguished from optimisation (which transforms) and engines (which assemble) by being read-only inspection. Usually ship as dashboards, IDE panels, or web apps.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • context-lens

docs.title: Data
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data

Data layer feeding agents — embeddings, RAG, vector stores, search, and grounding.

Infrastructure and pipelines that turn raw sources into retrievable, LLM-consumable data. Distinguished from Context (window assembly) and Memory (agent state) by focus on the underlying data plane. Includes embedding pipelines, vector DBs, semantic search, grounding, and storage.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Embedding Pipelines — Orchestrators that chunk, embed, and upsert content into vector stores on a schedule or event.
  • Grounding (3 sub) — Feeding agents fresh, authoritative external facts at inference time to reduce hallucination.
  • RAG (5 sub, 1 proj) — Retrieval-Augmented Generation stacks: engines, ingestion, rerankers, and KG builders that feed LLMs.
  • Semantic Search (1 proj) — Embedding-based search engines and libraries that match queries by meaning rather than keywords.
  • Storage (2 sub) — General-purpose data stores and backend query layers used by agents outside vector-specific workloads.
  • Vector Databases (1 proj) — Purpose-built stores for embeddings supporting ANN search, filtering, and hybrid retrieval.

docs.title: Embedding Pipelines
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Embedding Pipelines

Orchestrators that chunk, embed, and upsert content into vector stores on a schedule or event.

Covers pipeline tools that transform source data into embeddings and load them into vector databases, handling chunking, batching, and incremental sync. Distinct from Vector Databases (the store) and RAG Ingestion (end-to-end retrieval pipelines); this slot is the dedicated embedding ETL layer.

docs.title: Grounding
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Grounding

Feeding agents fresh, authoritative external facts at inference time to reduce hallucination.

Parent slot for data sources, grounding libraries, and search utilities used to anchor LLM output in verifiable references. Distinct from RAG (retrieval pipelines) and Semantic Search (query engines) by focusing on provenance and factuality rather than the retrieval mechanism itself.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Data Sources (1 sub) — Curated external datasets and feeds used as authoritative grounding material for agents.
  • Grounding Tools — Libraries that attach citations, provenance, and verification to LLM outputs.
  • Search Utilities — Lightweight search APIs and clients used by agents to fetch grounding snippets on demand.

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Grounding / Data Sources

Curated external datasets and feeds used as authoritative grounding material for agents.

Parent slot for upstream data providers (financial, news, reference) plugged into agents to ground answers. Distinct from Grounding Tools (the libraries) and Search Utilities (the lookup layer); this is the source side of the grounding stack.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Financial (1 proj) — Financial market and company data feeds used to ground agents in quantitative and fundamental data.

docs.title: Financial
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Grounding / Data Sources / Financial

Financial market and company data feeds used to ground agents in quantitative and fundamental data.

Data platforms and SDKs exposing equities, fundamentals, macro, and alternative financial data for retrieval by agents. Sits under Grounding/Data Sources as a domain-specific feed, not a generic API connector. Typically consumed as a Python library or REST API.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OpenBB

docs.title: Grounding Tools
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Grounding / Grounding Tools

Libraries that attach citations, provenance, and verification to LLM outputs.

Covers software that wraps LLM calls with source tracking, citation insertion, and factual verification against grounding corpora. Distinct from raw Data Sources and Search Utilities by focusing on the binding between generation and evidence.

docs.title: Search Utilities
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Grounding / Search Utilities

Lightweight search APIs and clients used by agents to fetch grounding snippets on demand.

Covers web/search APIs and helper libraries agents call to pull fresh evidence at inference time. Distinct from Data Sources (the underlying corpora) and Grounding Tools (citation binding); this slot is the query/fetch convenience layer.

docs.title: RAG
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / RAG

Retrieval-Augmented Generation stacks: engines, ingestion, rerankers, and KG builders that feed LLMs.

Parent category for tools implementing RAG pipelines — from document parsing and ingestion to retrieval engines, rerankers, and knowledge-graph construction. Distinct from raw Vector Databases or Semantic Search by covering the full retrieval-to-generation workflow used by agents.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Document Parsing for LLMs (4 proj) — Parsers that convert PDFs, Office files, and HTML into LLM-ready structured text and markdown.
  • Engines (1 proj) — End-to-end RAG engines that bundle parsing, indexing, retrieval, and generation into a single runnable service.
  • Ingestion (1 proj) — Pipelines that pull documents from sources and prepare them for embedding and indexing in a RAG stack.
  • Knowledge Graph Construction (3 proj) — Building entity-and-relation knowledge graphs from unstructured text to power graph-aware retrieval.
  • Rerankers (4 proj) — Models and services that reorder retrieved candidates by relevance before they reach the LLM.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Airweave

docs.title: Document Parsing for LLMs
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / RAG / Document Parsing for LLMs

Parsers that convert PDFs, Office files, and HTML into LLM-ready structured text and markdown.

Covers tools (Docling, Marker, LlamaParse, Unstructured) that extract clean text, tables, and layout from heterogeneous documents for downstream RAG ingestion. Distinct from general Ingestion pipelines; this slot is the parsing primitive feeding them.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Docling
  • LlamaParse
  • Marker
  • Unstructured

docs.title: Engines
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / RAG / Engines

End-to-end RAG engines that bundle parsing, indexing, retrieval, and generation into a single runnable service.

Integrated retrieval-augmented generation platforms exposing a server or library that handles the full pipeline from document ingest through grounded answer synthesis. Distinct from the standalone ingestion, reranker, and KG-construction siblings, which cover single pipeline stages. Typically deployed as a service with a query API.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • RAGFlow

docs.title: Ingestion
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / RAG / Ingestion

Pipelines that pull documents from sources and prepare them for embedding and indexing in a RAG stack.

Tools focused on the source-to-chunk stage of RAG: connectors, crawlers, chunking, and normalization before vectors are written. Excludes downstream retrieval, reranking, or full engines. Usually libraries or workers plugged into a larger RAG runtime.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent-Reach

docs.title: Knowledge Graph Construction
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / RAG / Knowledge Graph Construction

Building entity-and-relation knowledge graphs from unstructured text to power graph-aware retrieval.

Libraries that extract nodes and edges from documents and expose graph-based retrieval strategies as an alternative or complement to pure vector RAG. Sibling to plain ingestion and reranking, which stay in the vector-chunk paradigm. Typically a Python library writing to a graph or hybrid store.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Graphiti
  • LightRAG
  • Microsoft GraphRAG

docs.title: Rerankers
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / RAG / Rerankers

Models and services that reorder retrieved candidates by relevance before they reach the LLM.

Cross-encoder or late-interaction rerankers consumed as a hosted API or local model, inserted between a first-stage retriever and the generator. Distinct from embedding-based retrieval and full engines; concerned only with precision on a candidate list. Used as a library call or REST endpoint.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Cohere Rerank
  • FlashRank
  • Jina Reranker
  • MixedBread Rerank

docs.title: Semantic Search
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Semantic Search

Embedding-based search engines and libraries that match queries by meaning rather than keywords.

Tools and services for vector/semantic similarity search over code, docs, or arbitrary corpora. Distinct from Vector Databases (the backing store) and RAG (full generation pipelines); this slot is the query-side engine exposing search APIs or CLIs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • osgrep

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Storage

General-purpose data stores and backend query layers used by agents outside vector-specific workloads.

Parent slot for databases and query backends (SQL, NoSQL, object stores) that agents read and write through. Distinct from Vector Databases, which live in their own sibling; this covers the non-vector persistence substrate and its query interfaces.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Backend Querying (1 proj) — Querying layers that let agents and LLMs talk to existing databases and backends in natural language.
  • Databases (1 sub) — Databases used as persistent storage for agent data, documents, vectors, and context.

docs.title: Backend Querying
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Storage / Backend Querying

Querying layers that let agents and LLMs talk to existing databases and backends in natural language.

Federated query engines that sit over SQL, NoSQL, and SaaS backends and expose an LLM-facing interface, often as virtual tables or an MCP-style tool. Sibling to raw Databases: this slot is about the query federation layer, not the storage itself.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • MindsDB

docs.title: Databases
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Storage / Databases

Databases used as persistent storage for agent data, documents, vectors, and context.

Storage engines (vector, relational, graph, or hybrid) that back agent memory and RAG pipelines. Sibling to Backend Querying, which federates access; this slot houses the storage products themselves and specialized children like context databases.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Context Databases (1 proj) — Databases purpose-built for storing and querying agent context, sessions, and conversational state.

docs.title: Context Databases
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Storage / Databases / Context Databases

Databases purpose-built for storing and querying agent context, sessions, and conversational state.

Storage systems optimized for the structure of agent context—turns, tool calls, embeddings, and scoped memories—rather than general-purpose documents or vectors. Sibling-free under Databases; the slot exists to separate context-native stores from commodity vector or relational DBs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OpenViking

docs.title: Vector Databases
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Data / Vector Databases

Purpose-built stores for embeddings supporting ANN search, filtering, and hybrid retrieval.

Covers standalone vector database products exposing indexing, similarity search, and metadata filtering as a service or library (e.g., Qdrant). Distinct from Semantic Search engines that sit atop them and from general Storage; this is the persistence layer for embeddings.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Qdrant

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory

Persistent memory systems that let agents remember across sessions, users, and time.

Libraries and services providing long-term, episodic, or skill memory beyond the context window. Distinguished from Context (per-call assembly) and Data (source corpora) by stateful, agent-scoped retention. Typical interfaces: SDKs, memory-as-a-service APIs, or file-backed stores.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Episodic / Temporal Memory (2 proj) — Time-aware memory stores that record events and their temporal relations for agent recall.
  • File (1 proj) — File-backed memory formats that encode agent state into portable files such as videos or documents.
  • Memory-as-a-Service (3 proj) — Hosted memory backends exposing add/search/forget APIs so agents can outsource long-term recall.
  • Personal AI (1 sub, 2 proj) — Memory-centric personal assistants that retain per-user lifelong context across sessions and apps.
  • Regressive (1 proj) — Memory systems that iteratively refine or compress prior context through feedback loops.
  • Session State — Short-lived per-conversation state stores holding the active working context of a single session.
  • Skills (1 proj) — Reusable skill/procedure memories that agents can load to perform learned tasks on demand.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentMemory
  • Cognee
  • Mem0
  • MemOS

docs.title: Episodic / Temporal Memory
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Episodic / Temporal Memory

Time-aware memory stores that record events and their temporal relations for agent recall.

Covers libraries and graphs that model agent memories as time-stamped episodes or temporal knowledge graphs, enabling 'what happened when' queries. Distinct from Memory-as-a-Service (hosted generic memory) and Session State (per-conversation scratch); this is specifically temporal/episodic recall.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Graphiti
  • Zep Temporal KG

docs.title: File
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / File

File-backed memory formats that encode agent state into portable files such as videos or documents.

Tools that persist memory as self-contained files (e.g., Memvid encodes embeddings into video). Distinct from hosted Memory-as-a-Service and database-backed episodic memory; the defining trait is a file as the unit of memory storage and exchange.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Memvid

docs.title: Memory-as-a-Service
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Memory-as-a-Service

Hosted memory backends exposing add/search/forget APIs so agents can outsource long-term recall.

Managed services (Zep, Mem0, Letta Cloud) that provide durable agent memory behind an API, handling embeddings, storage, and retrieval. Distinct from self-hosted episodic or file memory by being delivered as a cloud product with SLAs and SDKs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Letta Cloud
  • Mem0 Platform
  • Zep

docs.title: Personal AI
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Personal AI

Memory-centric personal assistants that retain per-user lifelong context across sessions and apps.

Covers end-user personal AI products built around persistent individual memory — assistants, companions, and second-brain agents. Distinct from generic Memory-as-a-Service (developer APIs); this slot is the consumer-facing product layer that uses such memory.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Assistants (2 proj) — Personal AI assistants that maintain long-lived memory about a single user across sessions.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Crucix
  • Nanobot

docs.title: Assistants
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Personal AI / Assistants

Personal AI assistants that maintain long-lived memory about a single user across sessions.

User-facing assistants whose core feature is persistent, individualized memory and context rather than generic chat. Distinct from enterprise agents or task runners; these are deployed per-user, often self-hosted, and optimize for continuity over breadth.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Leon
  • OpenClaw

docs.title: Regressive
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Regressive

Memory systems that iteratively refine or compress prior context through feedback loops.

Experimental slot for context engines that learn from past interactions by regressing over accumulated state (e.g., Agentic Context Engine). Distinct from episodic recall or static stores by actively updating memory representations based on outcomes.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agentic Context Engine

docs.title: Session State
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Session State

Short-lived per-conversation state stores holding the active working context of a single session.

Covers mechanisms for persisting and restoring in-flight agent session data — scratchpads, turn history, transient variables. Distinct from long-term episodic or hosted memory services; this slot's lifetime is bounded by the session.

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Context, Memory & Data / Memory / Skills

Reusable skill/procedure memories that agents can load to perform learned tasks on demand.

Covers stores of executable or declarative 'skills' — reusable capability packets an agent can recall and apply. Distinct from episodic memory (facts/events) and session state; here the unit of memory is a procedure or competence.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Acontext

docs.title: Dev Tools & Skills
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills

Tooling, reusable skills, and prompt assets that developers use to build and operate agents.

Top-level domain covering engineer-facing artifacts: coding assistants and IDE integrations (Dev Tools), packaged agent capabilities (Skills), and prompt engineering assets (Prompts). Excludes runtimes, frameworks, and infra, which live elsewhere in the taxonomy.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Dev Tools (4 sub) — Developer-facing tools for writing, refactoring, debugging, and shipping code with AI assistance.
  • Prompts (7 sub) — Reusable prompt assets and tooling for authoring, managing, formatting, and optimizing LLM prompts.
  • Skills (5 sub, 1 proj) — Packaged, reusable agent skills—capability bundles with guides, registries, and interoperability standards.

docs.title: Dev Tools
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Dev Tools

Developer-facing tools for writing, refactoring, debugging, and shipping code with AI assistance.

Tools that embed LLMs into the software engineering loop: IDE plugins, autonomous SWE harnesses, refactoring assistants, and debuggers. Sibling to Skills and Prompts, which are reusable assets rather than interactive tools. Delivered as CLIs, IDE extensions, or standalone harnesses.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Coding Agent SWE Harnesses (3 proj) — Autonomous software-engineering harnesses that drive an LLM through multi-step code edits, runs, and tests.
  • Debugging (1 proj) — LLM-assisted debugging tools that help diagnose failing runs, logs, or models.
  • IDE Integrations (1 proj) — IDE and editor extensions that embed LLM coding assistance directly into the developer's editor.
  • Repo Refactoring (1 proj) — Tools that perform repository-wide refactors and structural rewrites using LLM-guided transformations.

docs.title: Coding Agent SWE Harnesses
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Dev Tools / Coding Agent SWE Harnesses

Autonomous software-engineering harnesses that drive an LLM through multi-step code edits, runs, and tests.

Agent loops specialized for the SWE task: read repo, plan, edit files, run tests, iterate. Distinct from passive IDE integrations, which keep the developer in the driver's seat, and from debuggers. Delivered as CLIs or local services that take a task and return a patch.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Aider
  • OpenDevin/OpenHands
  • SWE-agent

docs.title: Debugging
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Dev Tools / Debugging

LLM-assisted debugging tools that help diagnose failing runs, logs, or models.

Tools dedicated to fault diagnosis—stack traces, runtime telemetry, or model-internals inspection—using LLMs as the reasoning layer. Sibling to SWE harnesses (edit-loop oriented) and IDE integrations (authoring oriented). Typically invoked on an error artifact rather than a whole repo.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • vLLora

docs.title: IDE Integrations
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Dev Tools / IDE Integrations

IDE and editor extensions that embed LLM coding assistance directly into the developer's editor.

Plugins for VS Code, JetBrains, and similar editors providing inline completion, chat, and edit actions. Distinct from autonomous SWE harnesses—these keep the human in the loop—and from standalone CLIs. Shipped as editor extensions.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Roo-Code

docs.title: Repo Refactoring
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Dev Tools / Repo Refactoring

Tools that perform repository-wide refactors and structural rewrites using LLM-guided transformations.

Focused on large-scale, cross-file code changes—renames, migrations, architectural rewrites—rather than single-file edits or SWE task loops. Sibling to SWE harnesses and IDE plugins; this slot is specifically about refactor-as-a-workflow.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AgentRC

docs.title: Prompts
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts

Reusable prompt assets and tooling for authoring, managing, formatting, and optimizing LLM prompts.

Prompt engineering surface covering conversational control, formatting, workflow definition, management, structured output, and compiler-style optimizers. Distinct from Skills (packaged capabilities) and Dev Tools (interactive coding tools); this slot is about the prompt as an artifact.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Conversational Control (1 proj) — Frameworks for constraining and steering the conversational behavior of LLM agents via prompt rules.
  • Feedback Formatting (1 proj) — Shaping user or tool feedback into prompts so models incorporate it reliably on the next turn.
  • Formatting (1 proj) — Encoding schemes and token-efficient formats for representing data inside prompts.
  • Prompt Management — Tools for storing, versioning, and organising prompt libraries across projects and teams.
  • Prompt Optimisation & Compilation (3 proj) — Frameworks that automatically tune, rewrite, or compile prompts from higher-level program specifications.
  • Structured Output (1 sub) — Techniques and libraries for forcing LLMs to emit schema-valid structured data.
  • Workflow Definition (1 proj) — DSLs and libraries for declaring multi-step prompt workflows and procedures.

docs.title: Conversational Control
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Conversational Control

Frameworks for constraining and steering the conversational behavior of LLM agents via prompt rules.

Tools that define guardrails, flows, and allowed turns for conversational agents at the prompt layer. Distinct from formatting or workflow-definition siblings, which target output shape or task DAGs rather than dialog dynamics. Typically a library or DSL wrapping the model call.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Parlant

docs.title: Feedback Formatting
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Feedback Formatting

Shaping user or tool feedback into prompts so models incorporate it reliably on the next turn.

Utilities that structure critique, evaluation results, or tool errors into a form the LLM can act on. Distinct from general output Formatting (model-to-user) and Structured Output (schema enforcement); this slot is about the feedback signal flowing back into the prompt.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agentation

docs.title: Formatting
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Formatting

Encoding schemes and token-efficient formats for representing data inside prompts.

Libraries and specs—like TOON—that define compact or LLM-friendly serializations as alternatives to JSON/YAML in prompts. Distinct from Structured Output (enforcing model output shape) and Feedback Formatting (critique). Focused on how input data is rendered for the model.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • TOON

docs.title: Prompt Management
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Prompt Management

Tools for storing, versioning, and organising prompt libraries across projects and teams.

Systems that treat prompts as first-class artifacts: CRUD, versioning, tagging, and retrieval of prompt templates. Distinct from optimisation (which rewrites prompts) and formatting (which shapes a single prompt). Typically CLIs, web apps, or SDKs backing a prompt store.

docs.title: Prompt Optimisation & Compilation
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Prompt Optimisation & Compilation

Frameworks that automatically tune, rewrite, or compile prompts from higher-level program specifications.

Libraries that optimise prompts via gradient-like feedback, bootstrapped demonstrations, or programmatic compilation. Distinct from manual prompt management: the prompt is an output, not an input. Usually Python SDKs wrapping an LLM call with a training loop (DSPy, TextGrad, AdalFlow).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AdalFlow
  • DSPy
  • TextGrad

docs.title: Structured Output
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Structured Output

Techniques and libraries for forcing LLMs to emit schema-valid structured data.

Covers constrained decoding, grammar enforcement, and JSON/Pydantic coercion at inference time. Distinct from formatting (stylistic) and workflow definition (control flow). Typically SDKs that wrap model calls and guarantee parseable output.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Constrained Decoding Libraries (4 proj) — Libraries that constrain LLM decoding to a grammar, regex, or schema at token level.

docs.title: Constrained Decoding Libraries
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Structured Output / Constrained Decoding Libraries

Libraries that constrain LLM decoding to a grammar, regex, or schema at token level.

Low-level tools that modify sampling to guarantee outputs conform to a formal grammar (JSON Schema, CFG, regex). Examples: Outlines, Guidance, XGrammar, Instructor. Typically Python libraries hooking into local inference runtimes or wrapping provider APIs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Guidance
  • Instructor
  • Outlines
  • XGrammar

docs.title: Workflow Definition
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Prompts / Workflow Definition

DSLs and libraries for declaring multi-step prompt workflows and procedures.

Lets authors define the shape of a task — steps, branching, roles — as reusable prompt-driven workflows, separate from full agent orchestration frameworks. Usually a markup, YAML, or code-based DSL consumed by a runner.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Packmind

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills

Packaged, reusable agent skills—capability bundles with guides, registries, and interoperability standards.

Everything around 'skills' as distributable units of agent capability: bundle formats, package libraries, registries, authoring guides, and cross-vendor standards. Sibling to Prompts (raw text assets) and Dev Tools (interactive IDE tooling). Think plugin ecosystem for agents.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Bundles (1 sub) — Packaged, distributable collections of agent skills shipped as a unit.
  • Guides (1 proj) — Instructional skills that teach an agent how to do a task rather than expose code.
  • Package Library (1 proj) — Hosted libraries of reusable agent skills exposed as installable packages.
  • Registries (1 proj) — Indexes and directories for discovering published agent skills across sources.
  • Standards (1 proj) — Open specifications defining how agent skills are authored, packaged, and loaded.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent Skills

docs.title: Bundles
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills / Bundles

Packaged, distributable collections of agent skills shipped as a unit.

Bundles group multiple skills into a single installable artifact (e.g. a Claude Code skill pack). Distinct from registries (discovery) and package libraries (individual modules). Typically tarballs or repo layouts consumed by a skill loader.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Claude Code (1 proj) — Skill bundles specifically targeting the Claude Code harness.

docs.title: Claude Code
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills / Bundles / Claude Code

Skill bundles specifically targeting the Claude Code harness.

Collections of skills authored against Claude Code's skill format and loader conventions. A vendor-specific slot under Bundles. Typically git repos installable into ~/.claude/skills or a plugin marketplace.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Marketing Skills

docs.title: Guides
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills / Guides

Instructional skills that teach an agent how to do a task rather than expose code.

Prose/procedure-first skills loaded into context to shape agent behaviour — e.g. a 'taste' guide. Distinct from package libraries (executable modules) and standards (specs). Usually markdown documents consumed by skill-capable harnesses.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Taste Skill

docs.title: Package Library
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills / Package Library

Hosted libraries of reusable agent skills exposed as installable packages.

Provider- or platform-hosted catalogues where individual skills can be imported into an agent project (e.g. Vercel Skills). Distinct from registries (indexes across providers) and bundles (grouped packs). Typically an SDK or web console.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Vercel Skills

docs.title: Registries
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills / Registries

Indexes and directories for discovering published agent skills across sources.

Browsable listings or APIs that catalogue skills from many authors and platforms. Distinct from package libraries (a single provider's store) and bundles (packaged collections). Typically static sites, JSON indexes, or lightweight APIs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent Skills (tech-leads-club)

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
Dev Tools & Skills / Skills / Standards

Open specifications defining how agent skills are authored, packaged, and loaded.

Protocol- and schema-level work on what a skill is — its manifest, invocation contract, and interop. Distinct from registries (discovery) and guides (content). Typically a spec document plus reference implementations (e.g. AgentSkills).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AgentSkills

docs.title: Ecosystem
docs.text:
Ecosystem

Meta-layer covering non-tool entities in the agentic AI landscape: organisations, destinations, and uncategorised items.

Holds actors and places around the ecosystem rather than software. Sits outside the functional taxonomy of frameworks, tools, and runtimes. Used for companies, foundations, conferences, and holding pens for items awaiting classification.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Destinations — Physical and virtual destinations in the agentic AI world — conferences, hubs, venues.
  • Organisations (4 sub) — Entities shaping the agentic AI ecosystem: companies, foundations, consortia, and government bodies.
  • Uncategorized (1 proj) — Holding pen for items awaiting classification into the taxonomy.

docs.title: Destinations
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Destinations
