Physical and virtual destinations in the agentic AI world — conferences, hubs, venues.

Places people go to engage with the ecosystem: events, meetups, hubs, and community sites. Distinct from organisations (entities) and tools. Acts as a map of where the field congregates.

docs.title: Organisations
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Organisations

Entities shaping the agentic AI ecosystem: companies, foundations, consortia, and government bodies.

Umbrella for the organisational actors behind standards, funding, and policy. Distinct from destinations (venues) and projects (tools). Subdivided by organisation type.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Companies — For-profit companies operating in the agentic AI space.
  • Foundations (1 proj) — Non-profit foundations supporting agentic AI standards, research, or community.
  • Foundations & Consortia — Multi-member consortia and joint foundations coordinating agentic AI standards work.
  • Government Bodies — Government and public-sector bodies with roles in agentic AI policy or oversight.

docs.title: Companies
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Organisations / Companies

For-profit companies operating in the agentic AI space.

Commercial entities building, funding, or selling agentic AI products and services. Distinct from foundations (non-profit) and government bodies. Used to track notable vendors as ecosystem actors, not their individual products.

docs.title: Foundations
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Organisations / Foundations

Non-profit foundations supporting agentic AI standards, research, or community.

Independent non-profits such as the Agentic AI Industry Foundation. Distinct from for-profit companies and multi-party consortia. Tracked as ecosystem stewards rather than tool vendors.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agentic AI Industry Foundation (AAIF)

docs.title: Foundations & Consortia
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Organisations / Foundations & Consortia

Multi-member consortia and joint foundations coordinating agentic AI standards work.

Alliances bringing multiple organisations together around shared specs, protocols, or governance. Distinct from single-entity foundations and private companies. Typically publish specifications and reference implementations.

docs.title: Government Bodies
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Organisations / Government Bodies

Government and public-sector bodies with roles in agentic AI policy or oversight.

Regulators, agencies, and state-affiliated institutes shaping how agents are deployed and governed. Distinct from private companies and independent foundations. Tracked for their policy and standards influence.

docs.title: Uncategorized
docs.text:
Ecosystem / Uncategorized

Holding pen for items awaiting classification into the taxonomy.

Temporary bucket for projects or entities not yet assigned a functional slot. Should trend toward empty as the taxonomy is refined. Not a stable category.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agency Agents

docs.title: Frameworks & Runtimes
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes

Top-level domain for the software substrate that executes agents: frameworks, runtimes, harnesses, loops, and orchestration.

Covers everything an agent runs on or is built with — from authoring frameworks and step loops to orchestration engines, runtimes, and starter kits. Distinct from tools (capabilities the agent calls) and models. The plumbing layer of the stack.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Environments (2 sub) — Execution environments where agents run — operating systems, desktops, and other host surfaces distinct from the framework code itself.
  • Frameworks (8 sub, 8 proj) — Programming-model libraries and SDKs for building agents — the code-facing abstractions developers import to define agent behavior.
  • Harnesses (7 sub, 2 proj) — Wrappers that extend or control an existing agent/LLM loop — adding research, dispatch, checkpointing, recovery, or compression around it.
  • Loops (1 proj) — Minimal agent loop patterns — tight read-think-act cycles exposed as the primary abstraction rather than a full framework.
  • Orchestration (1 sub, 4 proj) — Coordination layers that route tasks across multiple agents, models, or workers — the conductor rather than the individual performer.
  • Pipelines (1 proj) — Declarative, staged data/agent pipelines — DAG-style flows that transform inputs into agent-driven outputs.
  • Runtimes (3 sub, 1 proj) — Execution substrates that host agent processes — sandboxes, policy engines, and data layers that enforce isolation and lifecycle.
  • Starter Kits (1 sub) — Opinionated scaffolds and templates for bootstrapping a new agent project — boilerplate plus conventions, not a framework.

docs.title: Environments
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Environments

Execution environments where agents run — operating systems, desktops, and other host surfaces distinct from the framework code itself.

Covers the substrate an agent inhabits: OS-level agent shells, remote desktop hosts, and similar runtime surfaces. Distinct from Runtimes (process/sandbox layer) and Frameworks (programming model); an environment is the place the agent perceives and acts within.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• OS (1 proj) — Operating-system-style environments purpose-built as agent hosts, where the OS itself exposes primitives for agent perception and action.
  • Remote Desktops (2 proj) — Hosted remote desktop environments that agents drive via screen/keyboard/mouse — cloud VMs packaged as agent workspaces.

docs.title: OS
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Environments / OS

Operating-system-style environments purpose-built as agent hosts, where the OS itself exposes primitives for agent perception and action.

OS-level environments that treat the agent as a first-class citizen — shell, filesystem, and process model shaped around agent use. Distinct from remote desktops (which reuse a standard desktop) in that the OS itself is the abstraction.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • agent-os

docs.title: Remote Desktops
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Environments / Remote Desktops

Hosted remote desktop environments that agents drive via screen/keyboard/mouse — cloud VMs packaged as agent workspaces.

Remote desktops provisioned for computer-use agents: a real OS GUI streamed over the network that an agent controls. Distinct from OS-level agent environments (custom substrates) — here the desktop is standard and the contribution is the hosting, isolation, and streaming layer.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Abacus Desktop
  • cua.ai

docs.title: Frameworks
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks

Programming-model libraries and SDKs for building agents — the code-facing abstractions developers import to define agent behavior.

Libraries, SDKs, and DSLs that provide the primitives (agents, tools, memory, loops) for authoring agentic systems. Distinct from Runtimes (execution), Orchestration (multi-agent coordination services), and Harnesses (wrappers around existing agents). Subdivided by language, modality, and design style.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• .NET (1 proj) — .NET-ecosystem agent frameworks — C#/F# libraries for building agents on the .NET runtime.
  • Computer Use (1 proj) — Frameworks specialized for computer-use agents — primitives for screen observation, GUI control, and desktop task loops.
  • Multiagent Orchestration (2 proj) — Frameworks whose core abstraction is a team of cooperating agents — roles, messaging, and group workflows as first-class primitives.
  • Multimodal (1 proj) — Frameworks for agents that natively handle multiple modalities — video, audio, and vision alongside text.
  • Optimisation (1 sub) — Frameworks focused on optimising agent execution — parallelisation, batching, and runtime efficiency of agent workloads.
  • Social Simulations (2 proj) — Frameworks for simulating populations of agents in social settings — generative agents, games, and behavioral experiments.
  • Stateful Design (1 proj) — Frameworks that treat persistent agent state and memory as the central design concern — long-lived agents with durable identity.
  • Voice (1 sub, 2 proj) — Voice-first agent frameworks — libraries built around realtime speech I/O, turn-taking, and telephony-style interaction.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentForge
  • AIOpsLab
  • AutoGen
  • EvoAgentX
  • II-Agent
  • Langflow
  • uAgents
  • Vercel AI SDK

docs.title: Computer Use
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Computer Use

Frameworks specialized for computer-use agents — primitives for screen observation, GUI control, and desktop task loops.

Libraries whose abstractions are shaped around driving a computer (screenshots, clicks, keystrokes, UI grounding). Distinct from general multi-agent frameworks and from computer-use models themselves; this is the developer-facing scaffolding for building such agents.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent-S

docs.title: Multiagent Orchestration
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Multiagent Orchestration

Frameworks whose core abstraction is a team of cooperating agents — roles, messaging, and group workflows as first-class primitives.

Libraries built around multi-agent collaboration patterns: role assignment, inter-agent messaging, task delegation. Distinct from the Orchestration segment (standalone coordination services) — these are developer frameworks you import to define the agents and their interactions in code.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• CAMEL
  • CrewAI

docs.title: Multimodal
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Multimodal

Frameworks for agents that natively handle multiple modalities — video, audio, and vision alongside text.

Agent libraries where multimodal I/O (video, audio, image streams) is a first-class concern rather than an afterthought. Distinct from voice-only or text-only frameworks; typical interface is an SDK with streaming media primitives wired into the agent loop.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • VideoSDK Agents

docs.title: .NET
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / .NET

.NET-ecosystem agent frameworks — C#/F# libraries for building agents on the .NET runtime.

Agent frameworks targeting the .NET platform, providing idiomatic C#/F# APIs for agents, tools, and memory. Sibling to language/modality-specific framework slots; grouped by host language because ecosystem conventions and package distribution differ meaningfully from Python/TypeScript.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • BotSharp

docs.title: Optimisation
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Optimisation

Frameworks focused on optimising agent execution — parallelisation, batching, and runtime efficiency of agent workloads.

Libraries whose contribution is making agent systems faster or cheaper to run: parallel execution of sub-tasks, batched tool calls, scheduling tricks. Distinct from Orchestration (routing) — optimisation frameworks shape how individual agent workloads execute, not how multiple agents coordinate.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Parallelisation (1 proj) — Framework-level optimisation techniques for running agent workloads in parallel to cut latency or cost.

docs.title: Parallelisation
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Optimisation / Parallelisation

Framework-level optimisation techniques for running agent workloads in parallel to cut latency or cost.

Parallelisation primitives inside agent frameworks — concurrent tool calls, fan-out/fan-in execution, parallel chain steps. Complements other optimisation strategies (batching, caching). Exposed as framework APIs or execution planners.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • EnsoAI

docs.title: Social Simulations
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Social Simulations

Frameworks for simulating populations of agents in social settings — generative agents, games, and behavioral experiments.

Libraries designed to instantiate many interacting agents for sociological, economic, or behavioral simulation. Distinct from multi-agent orchestration (which targets task completion): the deliverable is the simulated society itself, often for research or game design.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Concordia
  • Moltbook

docs.title: Stateful Design
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Stateful Design

Frameworks that treat persistent agent state and memory as the central design concern — long-lived agents with durable identity.

Libraries organized around stateful agents: memory hierarchies, self-editing context, and long-term persistence are first-class. Distinct from stateless chain-of-tool frameworks; typical interface is a server plus SDK where agents outlive a single request and accumulate memory across sessions.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Letta

docs.title: Voice
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Voice

Voice-first agent frameworks — libraries built around realtime speech I/O, turn-taking, and telephony-style interaction.

Frameworks whose primary modality is voice: streaming STT/TTS, barge-in, realtime pipelines, and telephony integration. Distinct from generic multimodal frameworks by their realtime-voice focus. Typical interface: an SDK plus a media server coordinating audio streams with LLM turns.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Realtime Voice Pipelines (3 proj) — Frameworks wiring STT, LLM, and TTS into low-latency duplex pipelines for conversational voice agents.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• LiveKit Agents
  • TEN Framework

docs.title: Realtime Voice Pipelines
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Frameworks / Voice / Realtime Voice Pipelines

Frameworks wiring STT, LLM, and TTS into low-latency duplex pipelines for conversational voice agents.

Toolkits for building realtime voice agents that stream audio in and out with interruption handling and turn-taking. Distinct from generic voice SDKs by orchestrating the full pipeline. Typically Python/Node libraries or managed services.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Pipecat
  • Vapi
  • Vocode

docs.title: Harnesses
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses

Wrappers that extend or control an existing agent/LLM loop — adding research, dispatch, checkpointing, recovery, or compression around it.

Harnesses sit around a base agent or model and add cross-cutting behaviors: long-horizon research, sub-agent dispatch, context compression, retry and recovery, checkpointing. Distinct from Frameworks (you build with) and Runtimes (you run on); a harness is what you bolt onto a model or agent to make it durable and task-coherent.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Checkpointing — Harness features that snapshot agent state mid-run so tasks can be paused, resumed, or branched without replaying from scratch.
  • Context Compression — Harness techniques that shrink an agent's growing context window — summarisation, pruning, and hierarchical memory for long runs.
  • Deep Research (1 proj) — Harnesses specialised for long-horizon, multi-step research tasks with iterative search, reading, and synthesis.
  • Platform Specific (1 sub) — Agent harnesses tightly coupled to a specific host OS or platform rather than being runtime-agnostic.
  • Retry and Recovery — Harness components handling failure detection, retry policy, and recovery from tool or model errors mid-task.
  • Sub-agent Dispatch — Harness logic for spawning, routing to, and collecting results from sub-agents under a parent controller.
  • Task Coherence — Harness features that keep an agent on-task across long runs, preventing drift and goal decay.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Hive
  • oh-my-openagent

docs.title: Checkpointing
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Checkpointing

Harness features that snapshot agent state mid-run so tasks can be paused, resumed, or branched without replaying from scratch.

Mechanisms for capturing and restoring an agent's working state — memory, scratchpad, tool history — at arbitrary points in a long task. Distinct from retry/recovery (which handles failure) and context compression (which shrinks state); checkpointing is about durable resumability.

docs.title: Context Compression
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Context Compression

Harness techniques that shrink an agent's growing context window — summarisation, pruning, and hierarchical memory for long runs.

Strategies and tools for keeping context usable as an agent accumulates history: rolling summaries, selective eviction, hierarchical notes. Distinct from general memory systems — this sits inside the agent loop to keep a single run within budget, rather than providing cross-session memory.

docs.title: Deep Research
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Deep Research

Harnesses specialised for long-horizon, multi-step research tasks with iterative search, reading, and synthesis.

Agent scaffolds that drive deep-research workflows: planning queries, fanning out to retrieval, reading sources, and composing reports. Distinct from generic sub-agent dispatch in that the harness is opinionated around research loops. Typically a library or runtime template layered over an LLM.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • DeepAgents

docs.title: Platform Specific
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Platform Specific

Agent harnesses tightly coupled to a specific host OS or platform rather than being runtime-agnostic.

Harnesses whose design assumes a particular platform (e.g. macOS, iOS, Windows) for native APIs, sandboxing, or UI control. In contrast to cross-platform harnesses, these trade portability for deep platform integration. Usually shipped as native apps or platform SDKs.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • macOS (1 proj) — Agent harnesses built specifically for macOS, leveraging native APIs and Apple silicon integration.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Platform Specific / macOS

Agent harnesses built specifically for macOS, leveraging native APIs and Apple silicon integration.

Harnesses that target macOS as a first-class host — using AppKit, Accessibility, or Metal/MLX for local inference and control. Shipped as native apps or Mac-only binaries. Sibling slot to other OS-specific harnesses under Platform Specific.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Osaurus

docs.title: Retry and Recovery
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Retry and Recovery

Harness components handling failure detection, retry policy, and recovery from tool or model errors mid-task.

Mechanisms that let an agent survive transient tool failures, malformed outputs, or stalled reasoning — backoff, re-planning, fallback paths. Sibling to checkpointing (which persists state) but focused on in-loop error handling. Exposed as harness middleware or runtime policies.

docs.title: Sub-agent Dispatch
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Sub-agent Dispatch

Harness logic for spawning, routing to, and collecting results from sub-agents under a parent controller.

Covers the dispatch layer that lets a primary agent delegate work to specialised sub-agents and merge their outputs. Distinct from general multi-agent orchestration in that it lives inside a single harness as a control primitive. Usually a library API for task fan-out.

docs.title: Task Coherence
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Harnesses / Task Coherence

Harness features that keep an agent on-task across long runs, preventing drift and goal decay.

Techniques for maintaining alignment between an agent's current actions and its original goal — goal reminders, plan re-grounding, scope guards. Complements context compression (which shrinks history) by ensuring intent survives the compression. Typically library-level scaffolding.

docs.title: Loops
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Loops

Minimal agent loop patterns — tight read-think-act cycles exposed as the primary abstraction rather than a full framework.

Projects whose central contribution is the iteration loop itself: a small, opinionated control flow that repeatedly prompts, acts, and observes. Distinct from Frameworks (which bundle many concerns) and Harnesses (which wrap existing agents). Useful as building blocks or reference implementations.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Ralph

docs.title: Orchestration
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Orchestration

Coordination layers that route tasks across multiple agents, models, or workers — the conductor rather than the individual performer.

Services and libraries that dispatch, schedule, and compose work across many agents or model endpoints. Distinct from multi-agent frameworks (which define the agents) in that orchestration focuses on routing, policy, and cross-agent flow. Typical interface: a controller service or SDK that agents and tools register with.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Platform-Specific (1 proj) — Orchestration frameworks tied to a specific cloud, vendor, or platform ecosystem.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Agent Orchestrator
  • AI Maestro
  • Conductor
  • Shannon

docs.title: Platform-Specific
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Orchestration / Platform-Specific

Orchestration frameworks tied to a specific cloud, vendor, or platform ecosystem.

Multi-agent orchestration offerings that assume a particular provider (e.g. AWS, Azure) for routing, state, and tool integration. Distinct from vendor-neutral orchestrators. Usually delivered as SDKs or managed services bound to that platform's primitives.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent Squad

docs.title: Pipelines
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Pipelines

Declarative, staged data/agent pipelines — DAG-style flows that transform inputs into agent-driven outputs.

Pipeline-oriented tooling for chaining extractors, transformers, and agent stages into a repeatable flow. Distinct from Orchestration (dynamic multi-agent routing) and Loops (tight iteration): pipelines emphasize staged, mostly linear transformations, often to construct agents or artifacts from source material.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Paper2Agent

docs.title: Runtimes
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Runtimes

Execution substrates that host agent processes — sandboxes, policy engines, and data layers that enforce isolation and lifecycle.

Runtime layers providing the OS-like concerns for agents: sandboxing, policy enforcement, persistent data. Distinct from Frameworks (developer API) and Environments (the surface the agent acts on); the runtime is what actually runs the agent process and governs what it can touch.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Data Layers (1 proj) — Runtime-adjacent storage and data-access layers that agents rely on for state, memory, and dataset I/O.
  • Policy Engines (1 proj) — Runtime components that evaluate and enforce permission, safety, or governance policies on agent actions.
  • Sandboxes (1 sub, 1 proj) — Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, shell commands, or untrusted tools safely.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OpenShell

docs.title: Data Layers
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Runtimes / Data Layers

Runtime-adjacent storage and data-access layers that agents rely on for state, memory, and dataset I/O.

Data substrates embedded in or paired with agent runtimes — versioned datasets, vector-ready lakes, append-only logs. Sibling to sandboxes (execution) and policy engines (authorisation). Usually a library or service exposing a dataset or storage API to running agents.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Deep Lake

docs.title: Policy Engines
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Runtimes / Policy Engines

Runtime components that evaluate and enforce permission, safety, or governance policies on agent actions.

Authorisation and guardrail engines invoked by an agent runtime before tool calls or state mutations. Distinct from data layers and sandboxes in that they answer allow/deny questions against declared rules. Typically embedded libraries or sidecar services.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Aegis

docs.title: Sandboxes
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Runtimes / Sandboxes

Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, shell commands, or untrusted tools safely.

Ephemeral, isolated compute for agent-driven actions, ranging from microVMs to container-based sandboxes. Distinct from policy engines (rules) and data layers (state) by providing an execution surface. Delivered as APIs, services, or embeddable runtimes.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Code Execution Sandboxes (4 proj) — Sandboxes purpose-built for running LLM-generated code with fast startup and per-session isolation.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • OpenSandbox

docs.title: Code Execution Sandboxes
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Runtimes / Sandboxes / Code Execution Sandboxes

Sandboxes purpose-built for running LLM-generated code with fast startup and per-session isolation.

Managed or self-hosted services that spin up disposable VMs/containers for each agent code-execution request, with filesystem, network, and package tooling. Distinct from general sandboxes by optimising for code interpreters. Consumed via SDKs and REST APIs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Daytona
  • E2B
  • Modal Sandboxes
  • Riza

docs.title: Starter Kits
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Starter Kits

Opinionated scaffolds and templates for bootstrapping a new agent project — boilerplate plus conventions, not a framework.

Ready-to-clone templates, reference apps, and scaffolds that get a developer from zero to a running agent quickly. Distinct from Frameworks (the library they wrap) in that a starter kit is a project skeleton, often specialized by domain (e.g. social simulations).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Social Simulations (1 proj) — Starter kits for building multi-agent social simulations with personas, worlds, and interactions.

docs.title: Social Simulations
docs.text:
Frameworks & Runtimes / Starter Kits / Social Simulations

Starter kits for building multi-agent social simulations with personas, worlds, and interactions.

Opinionated templates for populating a simulated environment with many agents that socialise, plan, and act — à la generative agents research. Distinct from general multi-agent orchestration by providing world, persona, and UI scaffolding. Usually a runnable sample project.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AI Town

docs.title: Interfaces
docs.text:
Interfaces

User-facing surfaces through which humans interact with agents — frontends, workspaces, UX patterns, and annotation tools.

Top-level domain covering everything a human sees or touches when working with agents: client apps, chat UIs, workspace environments, UX primitives like human-in-the-loop, and data annotation interfaces. Sits above the runtime and framework layers.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Annotation — Tools for labelling, annotating, and reviewing agent data — traces, outputs, training examples.
  • Frontends (9 sub) — Client applications that expose agent capabilities to end users across desktop, mobile, web, and terminal.
  • UX (1 sub) — Reusable UX patterns and primitives for agent interaction, such as human-in-the-loop approvals and inspection surfaces.
  • Workspaces (3 proj) — Multi-agent workspace environments where users manage, converse with, and orchestrate several agents in one place.

docs.title: Annotation
docs.text:
Interfaces / Annotation

Tools for labelling, annotating, and reviewing agent data — traces, outputs, training examples.

Interfaces where humans add labels, corrections, or judgements to agent inputs and outputs. Used for dataset curation, eval scoring, and RLHF-style feedback. Distinct from workspaces and frontends by being editorial rather than conversational.

docs.title: Frontends
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends

Client applications that expose agent capabilities to end users across desktop, mobile, web, and terminal.

End-user client software — chat apps, desktop clients, mobile apps, TUIs, web UIs — that front an agent backend. Distinct from workspaces (multi-agent environments) and UX primitives. Usually a shipped binary or web app talking to model/agent APIs.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Android (1 proj) — Android-native client apps for interacting with agents and LLMs on mobile.
  • Chat UIs (1 proj) — Chat-style frontends that render agent conversations as threaded message UIs.
  • Desktop Clients (4 sub) — Native or packaged desktop applications for interacting with agents on Windows, macOS, or Linux.
  • GUIs and CLIs (1 proj) — Hybrid frontends exposing the same agent through both a graphical interface and a command-line entry point.
  • Mobile Clients (2 sub) — Mobile apps (iOS/Android) for interacting with agents from phones and tablets.
  • Server Software — Self-hostable server backends that power agent frontends with multi-user state, auth, and persistence.
  • Terminal Clients — Terminal-resident clients that expose agents as a shell-style REPL or command.
  • TUIs and CLIs (1 proj) — Text-based interactive frontends combining terminal UIs with command-line invocation.
  • Web Clients — Browser-based frontends for agents, served as web apps or SPAs.

docs.title: Android
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Android

Android-native client apps for interacting with agents and LLMs on mobile.

Frontends packaged as Android applications, using native OS capabilities for notifications, background tasks, and device integration. Distinct from cross-platform mobile clients by targeting Android specifically. Distributed via APK or Play Store.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Operit

docs.title: Chat UIs
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Chat UIs

Chat-style frontends that render agent conversations as threaded message UIs.

Reusable chat interface components and apps designed specifically for agent interactions — message streams, tool-call rendering, and thread history. Distinct from platform-specific desktop/mobile/web clients: focuses on the chat UX paradigm itself, often as an embeddable library or shell over an underlying agent runtime.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent UI

docs.title: Desktop Clients
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Desktop Clients

Native or packaged desktop applications for interacting with agents on Windows, macOS, or Linux.

Installable desktop apps exposing agent chat, tools, and workflows through an OS-native shell. Unlike web clients, they run locally with filesystem and OS integration; subdivided by target platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, Cross-Platform).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Cross-Platform — Desktop agent clients built to run on Windows, macOS, and Linux from a single codebase.
  • Linux — Linux-native or Linux-first desktop clients for agents.
  • macOS — macOS-native desktop clients for agents.
  • Windows — Windows-native desktop clients for agents.

docs.title: Cross-Platform
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Desktop Clients / Cross-Platform

Desktop agent clients built to run on Windows, macOS, and Linux from a single codebase.

Electron/Tauri/Qt-style desktop apps targeting all three major OSes. Distinguished from per-platform siblings by a shared codebase and unified feature parity across operating systems.

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Desktop Clients / Linux

Linux-native or Linux-first desktop clients for agents.

Desktop agent applications specifically packaged for Linux — often via Flatpak, AppImage, or distro repos — or taking advantage of Linux-specific integrations. Distinct from cross-platform builds by being Linux-targeted primary releases.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Desktop Clients / macOS

macOS-native desktop clients for agents.

Agent desktop apps built for macOS, often leveraging Apple frameworks and shipped via DMG or the App Store. Distinguished from cross-platform builds by being Mac-only or Mac-first.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Desktop Clients / Windows

Windows-native desktop clients for agents.

Agent desktop apps distributed as Windows installers (MSI/EXE/Store) with Windows-specific integrations. Separated from cross-platform builds to capture tools that are Windows-only or Windows-first.

docs.title: GUIs and CLIs
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / GUIs and CLIs

Hybrid frontends exposing the same agent through both a graphical interface and a command-line entry point.

Tools that ship a GUI alongside a CLI, letting users drive the same agent interactively or from scripts. Distinct from pure TUI/terminal clients: here the GUI is a first-class surface, not an afterthought.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Goose

docs.title: Mobile Clients
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Mobile Clients

Mobile apps (iOS/Android) for interacting with agents from phones and tablets.

Native or hybrid mobile applications exposing agent chat and tools on handheld devices. Separated from desktop and web clients because mobile constraints (touch, push, background) shape the UX; subdivided by platform.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Android — Android apps for interacting with agents.
  • iOS — iOS apps for interacting with agents on iPhone and iPad.

docs.title: Android
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Mobile Clients / Android

Android apps for interacting with agents.

Agent clients packaged as APKs/AABs for Android phones and tablets, distributed via Play Store or sideload. Distinct from iOS siblings by platform SDK, distribution, and integration surface.

docs.title: iOS
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Mobile Clients / iOS

iOS apps for interacting with agents on iPhone and iPad.

Agent clients built with Apple's iOS SDKs and distributed via the App Store or TestFlight. Separated from Android siblings by platform, distribution, and sandboxing model.

docs.title: Server Software
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Server Software

Self-hostable server backends that power agent frontends with multi-user state, auth, and persistence.

Backend services that sit behind chat/web/desktop clients, providing accounts, conversation storage, model routing, and tool access. Unlike inference servers, their job is frontend-facing session and UX state, not raw model execution.

docs.title: Terminal Clients
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Terminal Clients

Terminal-resident clients that expose agents as a shell-style REPL or command.

Lightweight terminal programs for chatting with or dispatching agents from the command line. Simpler than full TUIs (no panels/keybindings) and distinct from GUI/desktop clients — the terminal is the entire surface.

docs.title: TUIs and CLIs
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / TUIs and CLIs

Text-based interactive frontends combining terminal UIs with command-line invocation.

Interactive TUI dashboards plus scriptable CLI entry points for agents, running fully inside a terminal. Distinct from pure terminal clients by offering richer panel/keybinding UIs, and from GUI+CLI hybrids by staying text-only.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent Deck

docs.title: Web Clients
docs.text:
Interfaces / Frontends / Web Clients

Browser-based frontends for agents, served as web apps or SPAs.

Hosted or self-hostable web applications providing chat and tool UIs to agents over HTTP. Distinct from desktop clients (no install) and from server software (which powers them): this slot is the browser-facing UI layer.

docs.title: UX
docs.text:
Interfaces / UX

Reusable UX patterns and primitives for agent interaction, such as human-in-the-loop approvals and inspection surfaces.

Design patterns and component libraries that shape how users supervise, approve, or correct agent actions. Distinct from full frontends by being pattern-level rather than application-level. Often delivered as UI libraries or reference implementations.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Human in the Loop (2 proj) — UX patterns and inboxes letting humans review, approve, or correct agent actions mid-run.

docs.title: Human in the Loop
docs.text:
Interfaces / UX / Human in the Loop

UX patterns and inboxes letting humans review, approve, or correct agent actions mid-run.

Tools providing approval queues, intervention points, and review inboxes where an agent pauses for human judgment before proceeding. Typically integrates with an orchestrator via webhooks or SDK callbacks; distinct from generic chat UIs by centering on gating and oversight workflows.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Agent Inbox
  • Orchestra

docs.title: Workspaces
docs.text:
Interfaces / Workspaces

Multi-agent workspace environments where users manage, converse with, and orchestrate several agents in one place.

Integrated environments that host multiple agents, conversations, and tools behind a unified UI — closer to an IDE or team workspace than a single chat client. Distinct from frontends (single-client) by being multi-agent and multi-session. Typically web apps or desktop workspaces.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentsMesh
  • Gastown
  • LobeHub

docs.title: Models & Inference
docs.text:
Models & Inference

The model layer: base models themselves and the infrastructure that serves them to agents.

Top-level segment covering both the models an agent calls (LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, synthetic data) and the inference plumbing around them (servers, gateways, routers, caches). Distinct from frameworks and tooling: this is where tokens are generated and routed, not where agent logic is authored.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Inference (6 sub) — Infrastructure that serves, routes, and optimises model calls between agents and model backends.
  • Models (5 sub) — The models themselves and tools for producing them — LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, and synthetic data.

docs.title: Inference
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference

Infrastructure that serves, routes, and optimises model calls between agents and model backends.

The serving plane: inference servers, gateways, proxies, routers, semantic caches, and structured-output enforcement that sit between an agent and the underlying model. Distinct from the Models sibling, which covers the models themselves rather than how requests reach them.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Gateways (1 sub) — Unified API gateways that front multiple model providers behind a single endpoint.
  • Inference Servers — Servers that execute model inference locally or on dedicated hardware.
  • Proxy (2 sub) — Transparent proxies that sit between agents and model APIs, forwarding requests while adapting protocols or endpoints.
  • Routers (3 sub) — Request routers that pick which model or provider should handle a given prompt based on cost, capability, or tool affinity.
  • Semantic Caching — Caches that reuse prior LLM responses when a new prompt is semantically similar to one already seen.
  • Structured Output Gateways (2 proj) — Gateways that enforce typed, schema-valid outputs from LLMs via constrained decoding, grammars, or retry loops.

docs.title: Gateways
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Gateways

Unified API gateways that front multiple model providers behind a single endpoint.

Services exposing one OpenAI-compatible (or similar) API that fans out to many providers, applying auth, quota, logging, and context compression. Distinct from routers (which pick a model per request) and proxies (which are thinner pass-throughs) by offering a full management plane.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Context Compression (1 proj) — Gateway-layer services that compress or prune context windows before requests reach the model.

docs.title: Context Compression
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Gateways / Context Compression

Gateway-layer services that compress or prune context windows before requests reach the model.

Middleware that shrinks prompts — via summarization, dedup, or token pruning — to fit budgets and cut cost. Deployed as an inline gateway component. Distinct from memory stores and from model-side KV compression.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Context-Gateway

docs.title: Inference Servers
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Inference Servers

Servers that execute model inference locally or on dedicated hardware.

Runtimes such as vLLM-style engines that load model weights and expose an HTTP/gRPC inference API. Distinct from gateways/routers (which forward to other backends) — inference servers actually run the model and generate tokens.

docs.title: Proxy
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Proxy

Transparent proxies that sit between agents and model APIs, forwarding requests while adapting protocols or endpoints.

Covers proxy-style middleware for LLM traffic: protocol bridging (e.g. MCP to OpenAI), endpoint translation, and drop-in API compatibility layers. Distinct from Gateways (policy/auth), Routers (model selection), and Inference Servers (actual execution).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Gateways (2 proj) — Unified LLM gateway proxies exposing many providers behind one API with auth, logging, and rate limiting.
  • MCP to OpenAI (1 proj) — Bridges that expose MCP servers as OpenAI-compatible tool endpoints.

docs.title: Gateways
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Proxy / Gateways

Unified LLM gateway proxies exposing many providers behind one API with auth, logging, and rate limiting.

Covers multi-provider proxies like LiteLLM and AgentGateway that normalize provider APIs and add ops features. Sit in front of all model traffic for an agent or org. Distinct from pure routers (decision logic) and protocol bridges.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentGateway
  • LiteLLM

docs.title: MCP to OpenAI
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Proxy / MCP to OpenAI

Bridges that expose MCP servers as OpenAI-compatible tool endpoints.

Proxies such as MCPO that translate between the Model Context Protocol and the OpenAI tool/function-calling API, letting OpenAI-style clients consume MCP tools. Distinct from generic LLM gateways.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • MCPO

docs.title: Routers
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Routers

Request routers that pick which model or provider should handle a given prompt based on cost, capability, or tool affinity.

Libraries and services that decide, per-request, which backend LLM to call. Typically exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint and dispatches based on heuristics, classifiers, or policies. Distinct from Gateways (auth/observability) and Proxies (protocol translation).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Cost Optimisation (1 proj) — Routers whose primary objective is minimising spend by dispatching to the cheapest adequate model or provider.
  • Intelligent (2 proj) — Capability-aware routers that classify prompts and dispatch to the best-suited model.
  • Tool Specific (1 proj) — Routers specialised for dispatching tool calls to the model best suited to execute them.

docs.title: Cost Optimisation
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Routers / Cost Optimisation

Routers whose primary objective is minimising spend by dispatching to the cheapest adequate model or provider.

Cost-aware routing layers that track per-token pricing and redirect traffic to cheaper backends when quality allows. Distinct from intelligent (capability-based) and tool-specific routers.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • RelayPlane Proxy

docs.title: Intelligent
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Routers / Intelligent

Capability-aware routers that classify prompts and dispatch to the best-suited model.

Semantic or learned routers (Semantic Router, Agentic Flow) that inspect intent or difficulty and pick a model accordingly. Distinct from cost-first and tool-specific routing.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Agentic Flow
  • Semantic Router

docs.title: Tool Specific
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Routers / Tool Specific

Routers specialised for dispatching tool calls to the model best suited to execute them.

Routing layers that look at the tool-use surface of a request and pick a model with strong performance for that tool or schema. Distinct from general capability or cost routers.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • ClawRouter

docs.title: Semantic Caching
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Semantic Caching

Caches that reuse prior LLM responses when a new prompt is semantically similar to one already seen.

Middleware that embeds prompts and returns cached completions on vector-similarity hits, cutting cost and latency. Usually deployed as a proxy or SDK in front of the model. Distinct from generic HTTP caches and from KV-cache reuse inside inference servers.

docs.title: Structured Output Gateways
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Inference / Structured Output Gateways

Gateways that enforce typed, schema-valid outputs from LLMs via constrained decoding, grammars, or retry loops.

Tools like BAML and TensorZero that wrap model calls to guarantee JSON/DSL-conformant responses, handling parsing, validation, and repair. Sit between the agent and the raw inference endpoint. Distinct from routing or caching gateways focused on dispatch or reuse.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• BAML
  • TensorZero

docs.title: Models
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models

The models themselves and tools for producing them — LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, and synthetic data.

Covers base and fine-tuned models an agent consumes (LLMs, embedding models) plus the pipelines that create them (trainers, RL, synthetic data generation). Sibling of Inference, which handles serving; this slot is about what gets served.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Embedding Models — Embedding models that turn text (or other modalities) into vectors for retrieval, clustering, and semantic search.
  • LLMs (2 sub, 1 proj) — Generative large language models used as the reasoning core of agents.
  • Reinforcement Learning (1 sub, 2 proj) — RL frameworks, algorithms, and environments used to train or post-train agentic models.
  • Synthetic Data — Pipelines and tools for generating synthetic training or eval data for agent models.
  • Trainers (1 sub, 1 proj) — Training and fine-tuning frameworks aimed at producing agent-capable models.

docs.title: Embedding Models
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / Embedding Models

Embedding models that turn text (or other modalities) into vectors for retrieval, clustering, and semantic search.

Covers released embedding models used by agents for RAG, memory, and similarity search. Distinct from LLMs (generative) and from the databases or pipelines that consume the vectors.

docs.title: LLMs
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / LLMs

Generative large language models used as the reasoning core of agents.

Frontier and open LLM releases positioned for agentic use, including variants tuned for specific use cases or agent workflows. Distinct from embedding models, RL policies, and the tooling that trains or serves them.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Agent Specific (1 proj) — LLMs purpose-built or tuned for agentic workflows — planning, tool use, multi-agent coordination.
  • By Use Case (1 sub) — LLMs grouped by the specific downstream use case they are optimised for.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Gemini 3.1

docs.title: Agent Specific
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / LLMs / Agent Specific

LLMs purpose-built or tuned for agentic workflows — planning, tool use, multi-agent coordination.

Models released with agent scenarios as the primary target, e.g. multi-agent variants of frontier models. Distinct from general-purpose LLMs grouped by use case.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Grok 4.20 Multi-Agent

docs.title: By Use Case
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / LLMs / By Use Case

LLMs grouped by the specific downstream use case they are optimised for.

Parent slot for LLMs released or tuned for particular task families (computer use, coding, etc.). Distinct from agent-specific models where agency itself is the target.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Computer Use (1 proj) — LLMs tuned specifically for computer-use agents that control a GUI, browser, or desktop.

docs.title: Computer Use
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / LLMs / By Use Case / Computer Use

LLMs tuned specifically for computer-use agents that control a GUI, browser, or desktop.

Models such as Fara released for screen-grounded action prediction and desktop/browser automation. Distinct from general vision-language models and from the harnesses that execute the actions.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Fara

docs.title: Reinforcement Learning
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / Reinforcement Learning

RL frameworks, algorithms, and environments used to train or post-train agentic models.

Covers RL libraries (Acme-style) and agent-focused RL runtimes (RLinf), plus environment suites for agent training. Distinct from supervised trainers and from the models themselves; sits upstream of deployed agents.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Agent RL Environments (3 proj) — Environment suites and harnesses for training and evaluating agents via reinforcement learning.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Acme
  • RLinf

docs.title: Agent RL Environments
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / Reinforcement Learning / Agent RL Environments

Environment suites and harnesses for training and evaluating agents via reinforcement learning.

Projects like SkyRL, Verifiers, and OpenEnv that provide standardized agent environments, reward signals, and rollout infrastructure. Distinct from RL algorithms themselves and from eval benchmarks used purely for scoring.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• OpenEnv
  • SkyRL
  • Verifiers

docs.title: Synthetic Data
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / Synthetic Data

Pipelines and tools for generating synthetic training or eval data for agent models.

Covers projects that produce LLM-generated datasets — instructions, trajectories, tool-use traces — used to fine-tune or evaluate agents. Distinct from trainers (which consume the data) and from eval frameworks (which score models).

docs.title: Trainers
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / Trainers

Training and fine-tuning frameworks aimed at producing agent-capable models.

Libraries and services for SFT, DPO, and agent-specific fine-tuning (e.g. tool-use). Covers end-to-end trainers like Agent Lightning. Distinct from RL frameworks and from hosted inference; sits between raw data and deployable checkpoints.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Tool-Use Fine-Tuning (2 proj) — Fine-tuning services and pipelines specialised in teaching models to call tools and APIs correctly.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Agent Lightning

docs.title: Tool-Use Fine-Tuning
docs.text:
Models & Inference / Models / Trainers / Tool-Use Fine-Tuning

Fine-tuning services and pipelines specialised in teaching models to call tools and APIs correctly.

Covers OpenPipe, Together fine-tuning, and similar offerings focused on tool-call trajectories and function-calling accuracy. Distinct from generic SFT trainers and from RL environments.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• OpenPipe
  • Together Fine-tuning

docs.title: Observability & Evals
docs.text:
Observability & Evals

Telemetry, evaluation, and operational monitoring for LLM and agent systems across their lifecycle.

Covers benchmarks, eval and experiment-tracking frameworks, runtime observability, and cost/ops monitoring for agents and LLM applications. In scope: tracing, judge frameworks, analytics, SRE-style tooling. Out of scope: safety policy enforcement (see Safety & Governance) and model training infra.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Agent Analytics — Product analytics tailored to agent behavior — task success, tool usage, session funnels, user interactions.
  • Benchmarks (3 sub, 2 proj) — Standardized task suites and datasets used to score agent or LLM capability under controlled conditions.
  • Evals and Experiment Tracking (5 sub, 2 proj) — Frameworks and services for running evals, tracing agent runs, and tracking experiments across model/prompt versions.
  • Operations (1 sub) — Day-two operational tooling for running agent and LLM systems in production — cost, budgets, SLOs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • RagaAI Catalyst

docs.title: Agent Analytics
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Agent Analytics

Product analytics tailored to agent behavior — task success, tool usage, session funnels, user interactions.

Dashboards and metrics pipelines that treat agent runs as the unit of analysis, surfacing trends in completion rates, tool calls, latency, and drop-off. Distinct from raw observability (traces) and evals (scored runs): the focus is aggregate product/usage insight over time.

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Benchmarks

Standardized task suites and datasets used to score agent or LLM capability under controlled conditions.

Parent slot for benchmark harnesses and datasets — software engineering, web navigation, general assistants, and more. Typical interface: a task runner plus grading script that produces a comparable score. Distinct from eval frameworks, which build custom in-house test sets.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• General Assistant Benchmarks (2 proj) — Benchmarks measuring broad, open-ended assistant capability across mixed reasoning, tool-use, and knowledge tasks.
  • Software Engineering Benchmarks (3 proj) — Benchmarks grading agents on code tasks — bug fixing, PR resolution, terminal workflows, freelance-style tickets.
  • Web Agent Benchmarks (3 proj) — Benchmarks that evaluate agents on browser and web navigation tasks in realistic or simulated sites.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AICGSecEval
  • OSWorld

docs.title: General Assistant Benchmarks
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Benchmarks / General Assistant Benchmarks

Benchmarks measuring broad, open-ended assistant capability across mixed reasoning, tool-use, and knowledge tasks.

Task suites like GAIA and AgentBench that probe general-purpose agent competence rather than a narrow vertical. Typically scored end-to-end on diverse real-world questions. Sibling to SWE and web-agent benchmarks, which focus on specific domains.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentBench
  • GAIA

docs.title: Software Engineering Benchmarks
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Benchmarks / Software Engineering Benchmarks

Benchmarks grading agents on code tasks — bug fixing, PR resolution, terminal workflows, freelance-style tickets.

Includes SWE-bench-style repo tasks, terminal-based shell tasks, and freelance/contract coding challenges. Typical interface: a dockerized repo plus test suite used as pass/fail oracle. Distinct from web-agent and general-assistant benchmarks.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• SWE-bench
  • SWE-Lancer
  • Terminal-Bench

docs.title: Web Agent Benchmarks
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Benchmarks / Web Agent Benchmarks

Benchmarks that evaluate agents on browser and web navigation tasks in realistic or simulated sites.

Harnesses like WebArena, VisualWebArena and Mind2Web that score agents on multi-step tasks across reproducible web environments. Typical interface: a sandboxed web stack plus task specs. Distinct from OS-level computer-use benchmarks and SWE benchmarks.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Mind2Web
  • VisualWebArena
  • WebArena

docs.title: Evals and Experiment Tracking
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Evals and Experiment Tracking

Frameworks and services for running evals, tracing agent runs, and tracking experiments across model/prompt versions.

Covers eval harnesses, LLM-as-judge runners, observability/tracing backends, time-travel debugging, and synthetic user simulators. Typical interface: SDKs that log runs plus a UI for comparison. Sits above raw logging and below product analytics.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Frameworks (1 proj) — General-purpose eval frameworks for authoring, running, and scoring test suites against LLM and agent outputs.
  • LLM-as-Judge Frameworks (3 proj) — Frameworks that use an LLM to grade outputs of another LLM or agent against rubrics or reference answers.
  • Observability (1 proj) — Tracing and telemetry backends that capture LLM/agent spans, prompts, and tool calls for live debugging.
  • Synthetic User Simulation (3 proj) — Tools that generate simulated users to exercise agents in multi-turn conversational or task scenarios.
  • Trace Replay & Time-Travel Debugging (3 proj) — Tools that replay captured agent traces step-by-step, letting developers branch, rewind, and re-run from any point.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• LangWatch
  • TruLens

docs.title: Frameworks
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Evals and Experiment Tracking / Frameworks

General-purpose eval frameworks for authoring, running, and scoring test suites against LLM and agent outputs.

Libraries such as DeepEval that provide assertions, metrics, and dataset runners for LLM/agent testing. Typical interface: pytest-like SDK or CLI. Distinct from LLM-as-judge frameworks (judge-specific) and observability (trace-centric).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • DeepEval

docs.title: LLM-as-Judge Frameworks
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Evals and Experiment Tracking / LLM-as-Judge Frameworks

Frameworks that use an LLM to grade outputs of another LLM or agent against rubrics or reference answers.

Tools like Promptfoo, Inspect AI, and Patronus that orchestrate judge prompts, rubrics, and scoring pipelines. Typical interface: YAML/DSL tests plus a judge model. Distinct from generic eval frameworks by centering on model-graded scoring.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Inspect AI
  • Patronus AI
  • Promptfoo

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Evals and Experiment Tracking / Observability

Tracing and telemetry backends that capture LLM/agent spans, prompts, and tool calls for live debugging.

Services like Langfuse that ingest structured traces from SDK instrumentation and expose a UI for run inspection, filtering, and drill-down. Distinct from eval frameworks (scoring) and time-travel debuggers (replay).

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Langfuse

docs.title: Synthetic User Simulation
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Evals and Experiment Tracking / Synthetic User Simulation

Tools that generate simulated users to exercise agents in multi-turn conversational or task scenarios.

Harnesses such as Tau-bench and Snowglobe that script or LLM-drive fake users for stress testing, regression, and red-teaming of conversational agents. Distinct from static benchmarks by producing dynamic, interactive dialogues.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Hugging Face Agents Course user sims
  • Snowglobe
  • Tau-bench

docs.title: Trace Replay & Time-Travel Debugging
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Evals and Experiment Tracking / Trace Replay & Time-Travel Debugging

Tools that replay captured agent traces step-by-step, letting developers branch, rewind, and re-run from any point.

Services like Braintrust, AgentOps Replay, and LangSmith that turn stored runs into interactive debug sessions — edit a prompt mid-trace and re-execute. Distinct from passive observability by enabling intervention and what-if replay.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• AgentOps Replay
  • Braintrust
  • LangSmith

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Operations

Day-two operational tooling for running agent and LLM systems in production — cost, budgets, SLOs.

Ops-facing concerns beyond eval quality: spend tracking, rate-limit management, SLO monitoring, on-call signals. Currently anchored by cost monitoring. Distinct from observability (traces/spans) and analytics (usage trends).

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Cost Monitoring (1 proj) — Tracking token spend, API costs, and budget alerts across models, agents, and tenants.

docs.title: Cost Monitoring
docs.text:
Observability & Evals / Operations / Cost Monitoring

Tracking token spend, API costs, and budget alerts across models, agents, and tenants.

Tools like AgentOps that attribute LLM/API cost to runs, users, or features and raise alerts on budget breaches. Typical interface: SDK hooks plus a billing dashboard. Distinct from general observability by centering on dollars and quotas.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • AgentOps

docs.title: Safety & Governance
docs.text:
Safety & Governance

Controls, policies, and oversight mechanisms that keep agents safe, compliant, and accountable in production.

Covers security, identity/auth, PII redaction, guardrails, and human- or agent-in-the-loop oversight for LLM-driven systems. Typical interface: middleware, policy engines, or review UIs. Distinct from observability: focus is enforcement and intervention, not measurement.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Agent in the Loop — Patterns where a supervisory agent reviews, approves, or moderates another agent's actions before execution.
  • Guardrails (1 sub, 1 proj) — Runtime filters and policy checks that constrain agent inputs, outputs, or actions against defined rules.
  • Human in the Loop — Workflows and UIs that route agent decisions to humans for approval, correction, or escalation.
  • Identity and Auth (1 sub) — Identity, authentication, and delegated-authority mechanisms for agents acting on behalf of users or services.
  • PII and Redaction — Detecting and redacting personal, sensitive, or regulated data from prompts, traces, and agent outputs.
  • Security (4 sub, 2 proj) — Security tooling for agent systems — sandboxing, red-teaming, control planes, and automated defense.

docs.title: Agent in the Loop
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Agent in the Loop

Patterns where a supervisory agent reviews, approves, or moderates another agent's actions before execution.

Architectures that insert an LLM reviewer — a safety critic or policy agent — into another agent's loop to approve tool calls or outputs. Distinct from human-in-the-loop (which routes to people) and static guardrails (which apply rules without reasoning).

docs.title: Guardrails
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Guardrails

Runtime filters and policy checks that constrain agent inputs, outputs, or actions against defined rules.

Includes input/output validators, refusal classifiers, command confirmation layers like Shellfirm, and policy routers. Typical interface: middleware wrapping model calls or tool invocations. Distinct from identity/auth and PII redaction, which solve narrower problems.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Policy Engines & Guardrail Routers (4 proj) — Libraries and routers that enforce content, safety, and behavioral policies on LLM inputs and outputs.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Shellfirm

docs.title: Policy Engines & Guardrail Routers
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Guardrails / Policy Engines & Guardrail Routers

Libraries and routers that enforce content, safety, and behavioral policies on LLM inputs and outputs.

Policy engines intercept prompts and responses to block unsafe content, enforce topic boundaries, and route based on rules. Typically embedded as a library or proxy in front of model calls (e.g. NeMo Guardrails, Llama Guard, Guardrails AI). Sits between the agent runtime and the model.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Guardrails AI
  • Invariant Labs
  • Llama Guard
  • NeMo Guardrails

docs.title: Human in the Loop
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Human in the Loop

Workflows and UIs that route agent decisions to humans for approval, correction, or escalation.

Review queues, approval gates, and annotator interfaces where a human signs off on risky agent actions or labels ambiguous cases. Distinct from agent-in-the-loop (LLM reviewer) and from pure HITL data labeling, which targets training rather than runtime oversight.

docs.title: Identity and Auth
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Identity and Auth

Identity, authentication, and delegated-authority mechanisms for agents acting on behalf of users or services.

Covers agent identity protocols, scoped credentials, OAuth flows for agents, and non-human identity management. Typical interface: identity providers, token brokers, or standards. Distinct from guardrails (content policy) and from generic IAM by focusing on agent-specific delegation.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Agent Identity Protocols (2 proj) — Protocols establishing verifiable identity, credentials, and authority for autonomous agents acting on behalf of users.

docs.title: Agent Identity Protocols
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Identity and Auth / Agent Identity Protocols

Protocols establishing verifiable identity, credentials, and authority for autonomous agents acting on behalf of users.

Open specifications for how agents authenticate themselves to services, carry delegated authority, and prove provenance — e.g. agent passports, agent payment protocols. Protocol-level standards, not SDKs; the plumbing for agent-to-service trust across organizational boundaries.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Agent Passport
  • AP2 (Agent Payments Protocol)

docs.title: PII and Redaction
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / PII and Redaction

Detecting and redacting personal, sensitive, or regulated data from prompts, traces, and agent outputs.

Tools and libraries that scan text for PII/PHI/PCI and mask or tokenize before it reaches models or logs. Typical interface: middleware or SDK filters. Distinct from general guardrails by targeting data privacy and compliance specifically.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Security

Security tooling for agent systems — sandboxing, red-teaming, control planes, and automated defense.

Covers the runtime and operational security layer for agents: isolating execution, detecting malicious inputs, enforcing policy at the control plane, and automating incident response. Distinct from Guardrails (content/policy filtering) and Identity (who the agent is) — this slot is about protecting the system from and around the agent.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Automation (1 proj) — Security automation and SOAR-style platforms adapted for agent workflows and incident response.
  • Control Planes (1 proj) — Centralized policy and governance control planes that enforce rules across fleets of agents and tools.
  • Red Team (1 sub, 1 proj) — Offensive security tooling for probing agents and LLMs for vulnerabilities, jailbreaks, and unsafe behaviors.
  • Sandboxing (1 proj) — Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, commands, or tools without risking the host.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• agent-scan
  • AgentGuard

docs.title: Automation
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Security / Automation

Security automation and SOAR-style platforms adapted for agent workflows and incident response.

Playbook-driven automation for triaging alerts, executing responses, and orchestrating security workflows where agents are either operators or subjects. Distinct from Control Planes (policy enforcement) and Sandboxing (isolation) — this slot is about scripted, repeatable security actions. Example: Tracecat.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Tracecat

docs.title: Control Planes
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Security / Control Planes

Centralized policy and governance control planes that enforce rules across fleets of agents and tools.

Administrative layers that set, distribute, and enforce security policy across many agents — access control, audit, kill-switches. Sits above individual guardrail libraries, providing org-level governance. Example: Cordum.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Cordum

docs.title: Red Team
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Security / Red Team

Offensive security tooling for probing agents and LLMs for vulnerabilities, jailbreaks, and unsafe behaviors.

Red-team frameworks that attack agent systems to surface weaknesses before adversaries do — fuzzing prompts, chaining exploits, simulating malicious tool use. Distinct from evals (which measure capability) in that the goal is adversarial discovery.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Prompt Injection Scanners (3 proj) — Scanners and test harnesses specifically targeting prompt injection and LLM jailbreak vulnerabilities.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Rogue

docs.title: Prompt Injection Scanners
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Security / Red Team / Prompt Injection Scanners

Scanners and test harnesses specifically targeting prompt injection and LLM jailbreak vulnerabilities.

Tools that automate discovery of prompt-injection weaknesses in LLM applications via curated attack libraries and fuzzing (Garak, PyRIT, Promptfoo Redteam). A narrow slot under Red Team focused on the input-manipulation attack class, not broader adversarial ML.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Garak
  • Promptfoo Redteam
  • PyRIT

docs.title: Sandboxing
docs.text:
Safety & Governance / Security / Sandboxing

Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, commands, or tools without risking the host.

Lightweight VMs, microVMs, or container-based sandboxes purpose-built for agent code execution and tool invocation. Distinct from general dev sandboxes — optimized for short-lived, untrusted LLM-generated workloads. Example: Microsandbox.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Microsandbox

docs.title: Tools & Integrations
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations

Tools, actions, and integration layers that connect agents to the outside world — MCP, standards, and action surfaces.

The top-level domain for everything that extends an agent's reach: tool protocols (MCP), standards bodies, and the concrete action surfaces agents invoke (browsers, APIs, wallets, voice). Sits between frameworks/runtimes and the real-world systems agents manipulate.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• Actions (13 sub) — Concrete action surfaces agents invoke — browsers, computer use, APIs, wallets, voice, and third-party integrations.
  • MCP (4 sub) — Model Context Protocol ecosystem — servers, gateways, aggregators, registries, and third-party MCP implementations.
  • Standards (3 sub) — Open standards and specifications for agent interoperability — ratified, proposed, and ecosystem-tied protocols.

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions

Concrete action surfaces agents invoke — browsers, computer use, APIs, wallets, voice, and third-party integrations.

The "hands" of the agent: the actual effectors that let it browse, click, pay, extract, or call services. Distinct from MCP (the protocol) and Standards (the specs) — this slot is where real-world capabilities live, grouped by modality and target system.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• AI Browsers (1 sub, 1 proj) — Full browser products with native AI/agent capabilities built into the user-facing application.
  • Authenticated Tool Proxies (3 proj) — Hosted proxies that handle OAuth, credentials, and tool invocation on behalf of agents across many SaaS APIs.
  • Browser Automation (1 sub, 1 proj) — Programmatic browser control stacks aimed at agent-driven automation and scraping workflows.
  • Browser Tooling (2 proj) — Libraries and helpers layered on browser automation stacks to make them agent-friendly (DOM extraction, action planning).
  • Computer Use (3 sub, 1 proj) — Frameworks letting agents control a full desktop OS via keyboard, mouse, and screen — beyond just the browser.
  • Data Extraction and Structuring (1 proj) — Services that turn web pages and unstructured sources into clean, structured data for agents.
  • Financial Tools (2 sub, 2 proj) — Toolkits giving agents financial capabilities — wallets, payment rails, and transaction execution.
  • Integrations (1 proj) — Generic integration/connector libraries exposing many third-party APIs as agent-callable tools.
  • Marketplaces (2 proj) — Marketplaces where agents and their actions are listed, discovered, and transacted by other agents or humans.
  • Software Extensions (1 proj) — Extensions that embed agentic actions into existing host software such as IDEs, notebooks, or editors.
  • Upserting Tools — Tools that let agents insert or update records in external systems (CRMs, databases, SaaS) as a write-side action.
  • Voice (1 proj) — Voice action tools letting agents place calls, speak, and handle telephony-grade audio interactions.
  • Web Search APIs for Agents (4 proj) — Web search APIs purpose-built as agent tool calls, returning LLM-friendly results rather than SERP HTML.

docs.title: AI Browsers
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / AI Browsers

Full browser products with native AI/agent capabilities built into the user-facing application.

End-user browsers (Chromium forks or new builds) where agentic features are first-class — not scriptable automation stacks but actual browsers a human or agent drives. Distinct from Browser Automation (programmatic control) and Browser Tooling (libraries). Example: BrowserOS.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Headless (2 proj) — Headless browsers designed for AI agents — no UI, optimized for programmatic navigation and scraping.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • BrowserOS

docs.title: Headless
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / AI Browsers / Headless

Headless browsers designed for AI agents — no UI, optimized for programmatic navigation and scraping.

Browser engines built or tuned for agent workloads, exposing DOM/automation APIs without a rendered UI. In scope: Lightpanda, Steel Browser and similar headless runtimes. Out of scope: cloud browser infrastructure services and human-facing AI browsers.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Lightpanda Browser
  • Steel Browser

docs.title: Authenticated Tool Proxies
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Authenticated Tool Proxies

Hosted proxies that handle OAuth, credentials, and tool invocation on behalf of agents across many SaaS APIs.

Services that abstract auth and API surfaces so agents can call Gmail, Slack, Notion, etc. without managing tokens or per-API SDKs. Distinct from Integrations (generic connector libs) — the differentiator is managed auth and a unified tool surface. Examples: Arcade, Pica, Nango.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Arcade.dev
  • Nango
  • Pica

docs.title: Browser Automation
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Browser Automation

Programmatic browser control stacks aimed at agent-driven automation and scraping workflows.

Automation frameworks and runtimes that drive real browsers under agent control — built on or beside Playwright/CDP. Distinct from AI Browsers (user-facing products) and Browser Tooling (helper libs layered on top). Includes cloud browser infrastructure as a child.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • Cloud Browser Infrastructure (3 proj) — Hosted browser infrastructure providing managed sessions, proxies, and captchas for agent automation at scale.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Vibium

docs.title: Cloud Browser Infrastructure
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Browser Automation / Cloud Browser Infrastructure

Hosted browser infrastructure providing managed sessions, proxies, and captchas for agent automation at scale.

Cloud services running fleets of real browsers that agents drive via Playwright/CDP, handling scaling, auth, and anti-bot concerns. In scope: Browserbase, Hyperbrowser, Anchor Browser. Out of scope: local headless browser engines and human-facing AI browsers.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Anchor Browser
  • Browserbase
  • Hyperbrowser

docs.title: Browser Tooling
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Browser Tooling

Libraries and helpers layered on browser automation stacks to make them agent-friendly (DOM extraction, action planning).

Thin layers above Playwright/CDP that give agents structured affordances — accessibility snapshots, element selection, observation primitives. Distinct from full Browser Automation runtimes; these are the glue libraries agents use. Examples: Stagehand, Agent Browser.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• Agent Browser
  • Stagehand

docs.title: Computer Use
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Computer Use

Frameworks letting agents control a full desktop OS via keyboard, mouse, and screen — beyond just the browser.

OS-level agent control: platform-specific drivers, CLI-first approaches, and vision-based UI models. Distinct from Browser Automation in that the surface is the entire desktop/VM, not a web page. Example: cua.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text:
• CLI (1 proj) — Computer-use agents that drive the machine via CLI/shell rather than GUI interactions.
  • Platform Specific (1 sub) — Computer-use tooling scoped to a specific operating system, leveraging native APIs for control.
  • Vision UI (1 proj) — Vision-based computer-use agents that interact with GUIs through screenshots and pixel-level grounding.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • cua

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Computer Use / CLI

Computer-use agents that drive the machine via CLI/shell rather than GUI interactions.

Runtimes that let agents operate a computer primarily through terminal commands and shell sessions. In scope: CLI-first computer-use harnesses. Out of scope: vision/GUI computer use and OS-specific GUI controllers.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • usecomputer

docs.title: Platform Specific
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Computer Use / Platform Specific

Computer-use tooling scoped to a specific operating system, leveraging native APIs for control.

OS-bound computer-use implementations that rely on platform accessibility, scripting, or automation APIs. In scope: macOS-, Windows-, or Linux-specific controllers. Out of scope: cross-platform vision-UI or CLI-based computer use.

docs.title: Subcategories
docs.text: • macOS (2 proj) — macOS-native computer-use runtimes controlling the Mac via AppleScript, accessibility, or native APIs.

docs.title: macOS
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Computer Use / Platform Specific / macOS

macOS-native computer-use runtimes controlling the Mac via AppleScript, accessibility, or native APIs.

Agent harnesses that drive macOS specifically, using platform automation to control apps and windows. In scope: macOS-use, cmux and similar. Out of scope: cross-platform vision-based computer use and CLI-only harnesses.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
• cmux
  • macOS-use

docs.title: Vision UI
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Computer Use / Vision UI

Vision-based computer-use agents that interact with GUIs through screenshots and pixel-level grounding.

Systems using multimodal models to see the screen and emit clicks/keystrokes without DOM or accessibility access. In scope: vision-grounded GUI agents like UI-Venus. Out of scope: CLI-based and platform-native accessibility-driven computer use.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • UI-Venus

docs.title: Data Extraction and Structuring
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Data Extraction and Structuring

Services that turn web pages and unstructured sources into clean, structured data for agents.

Crawl-and-extract APIs that convert HTML, PDFs, and messy pages into markdown or JSON for LLM consumption. Typically a hosted API (Firecrawl) rather than a library. Distinct from Browser Automation — the goal is data output, not interaction.

docs.title: Projects
docs.text: • Firecrawl

docs.title: Financial Tools
docs.text:
Tools & Integrations / Actions / Financial Tools

Toolkits giving agents financial capabilities — wallets, payment rails, and transaction execution.
